class_schedule.topic: Managerial Accounting, Cost Terminology, and the Manager's Decision Problem
learning_objectives: Distinguish managerial accounting from financial accounting in purpose, audience, format, and regulatory environment
Classify costs by function (product vs period), behavior (variable vs fixed), traceability (direct vs indirect), and relevance to decisions
Explain the role of the managerial accountant in planning, directing, controlling, and decision making
Identify the ethical standards published by the Institute of Management Accountants (IMA) and apply them to common dilemmas
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 1 - Managerial Accounting and Cost Concepts
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting: A Managerial Emphasis (16th ed.), Ch. 2 - An Introduction to Cost Terms and Purposes
IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice (imanet.org)

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial accounting, which you studied in BA102, answers the question that external users ask: Is this a good company to invest in, lend to, or buy from? Managerial accounting answers a different set of questions, asked by the managers who run the company. Should we manufacture this component ourselves or buy it from a supplier? What should we charge for a new product? Should we keep the Cleveland plant open if it lost money last year? Is the sales manager hitting her targets? Is the European division earning enough to justify its investment? None of these questions are answered by financial accounting. In fact, GAAP-compliant financial statements actively get in the way: they absorb fixed manufacturing overhead into inventory, they aggregate costs at the corporate level that managers need broken out by product and territory, they treat R&D as a period expense when managers need it allocated to the products it supports. Managerial accounting exists because managers cannot run a business from the income statement alone. The stakes are concrete. Harley-Davidson nearly went bankrupt in the 1980s partly because its traditional costing system was quietly subsidizing expensive small-volume motorcycles with money earned on its high-volume Sportster line, which the cost system made look unprofitable. Activity-based costing, introduced in the mid-1980s, would eventually let Harley see the real picture. Hospitals, airlines, and manufacturers have made similar discoveries about which products and which customers actually make money when the cost system stops lying. This course will teach you how to design cost systems that tell the truth, how to use them to make decisions, and how to catch the judgment errors that let costs mislead management.
key_concepts: Managerial vs financial accounting: managerial serves internal users (managers), is forward-looking, custom-formatted, uses relevant data regardless of GAAP, emphasizes detail at the product/department/customer level, and operates on whatever time cycle management needs

Product costs (inventoriable costs): costs attached to units produced — direct materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead — that flow through inventory accounts and become COGS when units are sold
Period costs (non-inventoriable): selling and administrative expenses that are expensed in the period incurred and never touch inventory
Direct materials: raw materials that can be economically traced to a specific unit — the steel in a car, the microchip in a phone
Direct labor: wages of workers who can be economically traced to specific units — the assembler on the line, not the janitor or the plant manager

Manufacturing overhead: all manufacturing costs other than direct materials and direct labor — indirect materials (glue, screws), indirect labor (supervisors), factory depreciation, plant utilities, property tax on the factory, machine maintenance

Cost behavior: variable (moves proportionally with activity, e.g., DM per unit), fixed (unchanged over relevant range, e.g., plant rent), mixed (semi-variable, e.g., utility bill with fixed meter charge plus per-kWh charge)
Relevant range: the level of activity over which a cost behavior assumption holds; a fixed cost is only fixed within its relevant range — double volume and you may need a second plant

Traceability: direct costs can be traced to a cost object economically; indirect costs must be allocated through an allocation base; the same cost can be direct or indirect depending on the cost object

Cost object: anything for which a separate measurement of cost is desired — a product, a batch, a customer, a division, a marketing campaign — choice of cost object determines direct/indirect classification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a Ford F-150 and classify every cost category. Steel, aluminum, glass, tires, engine, transmission go to direct materials. Assembly-line worker wages go to direct labor. Plant manager salary, factory depreciation, welding robot maintenance, factory electricity go to manufacturing overhead. Advertising, dealer incentives, corporate headquarters go to period costs. Then reclassify the same costs using cost behavior: direct materials and direct labor are variable; factory rent and plant manager salary are fixed; electricity is mixed (fixed demand charge plus variable usage). Finally, consider the cost object. If the cost object is a single F-150, the plant manager's salary is indirect. If the cost object is the Dearborn plant as a whole, the plant manager's salary is direct. Students often miss that traceability depends on the cost object choice, not on the cost itself.
setup_requirements: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice handout

review.value: There is no managerial equivalent of GAAP; managers choose formats and methods that serve decisions; this freedom is both a blessing (flexibility) and a curse (inconsistency) and requires judgment

The same cost can be direct or indirect, fixed or variable, product or period depending on the cost object and the time horizon; always state your cost object and your relevant range before classifying

Advertising, executive salaries, and R&D are period costs and never flow through inventory; this is a GAAP rule but it can distort managerial decisions if taken too literally, because these costs do support product value

The IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice binds CMAs to competence, confidentiality, integrity, and credibility; managerial accountants face pressure to cook the books in ways financial accountants do not see, and the profession's credibility rests on resisting it

class_schedule.topic: Cost Behavior Analysis: Variable, Fixed, and Mixed Costs
learning_objectives: Define variable, fixed, step, and mixed cost behavior and graph each type
Apply the high-low method to separate a mixed cost into fixed and variable components
Apply linear regression to cost estimation and interpret R-squared
Evaluate the relevant range of a cost behavior assumption and identify when the assumption breaks
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 5 - Cost-Volume-Profit Relationships (cost behavior section)
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 10 - Determining How Costs Behave
Kaplan & Atkinson, Advanced Management Accounting (3rd ed.), Ch. 4 - Cost Estimation

lecture_notes.introduction: Every important managerial accounting technique — break-even analysis, contribution margin reporting, flexible budgeting, variance analysis — rests on knowing how costs behave with respect to activity. Yet most cost accounts in a general ledger are mixed, containing both fixed and variable components. The electric bill has a fixed demand charge and a variable kWh charge. The maintenance budget has fixed salaried technicians and variable parts and contract labor. Even direct labor, which textbooks call purely variable, is actually step-fixed in the short run because workers are hired and laid off in chunks. The managerial accountant's first job is to decompose these mixed costs into their fixed and variable components so that the resulting model predicts what costs will be at different activity levels. The stakes are real. Southwest Airlines famously built its low-cost model on understanding which costs scaled with flights (fuel, landing fees, hourly crew costs) versus which costs scaled with the fleet (maintenance, aircraft depreciation, pilot salaries). This cost model let Southwest price routes aggressively and still make money, because it knew which costs it could actually avoid by dropping a flight. Delta, which for years underestimated its fixed costs, frequently added flights that destroyed operating profit because the incremental revenue did not cover even the short-run variable costs, let alone contribute to fixed costs. Cost behavior is the hinge on which operational strategy turns, and getting it wrong is one of the fastest paths to losing money.
key_concepts: Variable cost: total cost changes proportionally with activity; per-unit cost is constant within the relevant range; examples include direct materials, sales commissions, per-unit royalties
Fixed cost: total cost is constant over the relevant range; per-unit fixed cost declines as volume rises; examples include rent, straight-line depreciation, salaried employee wages
Step-fixed cost: fixed over a narrow range then jumps to a new level; examples include supervisory salaries (one supervisor per 20 workers) and quality inspectors

Mixed (semi-variable) cost: contains both a fixed component and a variable component; the most common pattern in real general ledgers; utilities, maintenance, and equipment rentals with base plus usage fees
Cost function: Y = a + bX where Y is total cost, a is fixed cost, b is variable cost per unit, and X is activity level; the workhorse model of cost estimation

High-low method: pick the highest and lowest activity observations, compute slope as (high cost - low cost) / (high activity - low activity) to get variable rate, then back out fixed cost; fast but fragile because it uses only two data points

Regression analysis: use least squares to fit Y = a + bX using all data points; Excel's SLOPE, INTERCEPT, and LINEST functions; produces R-squared measuring fit quality and standard errors measuring uncertainty
R-squared: percentage of variation in Y explained by X; above 0.80 generally acceptable for cost estimation; below 0.50 suggests the activity driver is wrong
Relevant range: the activity range over which the cost behavior assumption is valid; outside it, fixed costs step up, capacity constraints bind, and variable rates may change

Engineering method: estimate costs from physical relationships (materials required per unit, labor minutes per unit) rather than historical data; used when history is unavailable or unreliable, e.g., new product launches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Given 12 months of data for a factory's total maintenance cost and machine hours, separate the mixed cost using both high-low and regression. High month: 10,000 hours, $48,000 cost. Low month: 4,000 hours, $24,000 cost. High-low slope = ($48,000 - $24,000) / (10,000 - 4,000) = $4 per hour variable. Fixed = $48,000 - ($4 × 10,000) = $8,000 per month. Cost function: Y = $8,000 + $4X. Now run the same data through Excel regression with all 12 points. Regression yields Y = $9,200 + $3.85X with R-squared = 0.92. Notice that regression uses all data and smooths out the influence of any single month. Apply both models to predict costs at 7,000 hours: high-low gives $36,000, regression gives $36,150. Discuss which to trust when the numbers diverge more dramatically (regression, almost always) and why high-low is still taught (sometimes you only have two months of data for a new product line, and you need a number).
setup_requirements: Scatter plot for visual inspection of linearity
Calculator or Excel for regression practice

review.value: Pure variable and pure fixed costs are the exception in practice; the core analytic task is decomposing mixed costs into their two components using an appropriate method

review.key: High-Low Uses Two Points, Regression Uses All
review.value: High-low is simple and fast but throws away information; regression uses every data point and produces diagnostic statistics; when the two methods disagree meaningfully, trust regression and investigate why

review.key: Relevant Range Is a Hard Constraint
review.value: A cost function is only valid inside the range of activity from which it was estimated; predicting costs at 150% of historical capacity using the same model is wrong because new fixed costs step in

If R-squared is low, the problem is usually that the activity driver does not explain the cost; utilities driven by machine hours make sense but utilities driven by units when products differ in energy intensity will fit poorly

class_schedule.topic: Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis: Break-Even, Contribution Margin, and Target Profit
learning_objectives: Compute contribution margin per unit, contribution margin ratio, and total contribution margin
Calculate break-even point in units and sales dollars for single-product and multiproduct firms
Compute sales required to achieve a target profit, before and after tax
Compute the margin of safety and operating leverage and interpret their managerial meaning
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 5 - Cost-Volume-Profit Relationships
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 3 - Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis
HBR, 'Break-Even Analysis: What and How' (classic)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost-volume-profit analysis is the single most used tool in managerial accounting because it answers the most common question managers ask: at what volume do we make money? Imagine you are a startup founder pitching a subscription box business. Your variable cost is $22 per box for contents and shipping. Your fixed costs — office rent, software, three employees — total $180,000 per year. You plan to charge $40 per box. Each subscription contributes $40 - $22 = $18 toward covering fixed costs. Once fixed costs are covered, every additional subscription contributes $18 to profit. The break-even calculation is simple: $180,000 / $18 = 10,000 subscriptions per year. Below 10,000 you lose money; above it you make money. This single number — 10,000 — should shape every decision you make, from whether to rent a bigger office to whether you can afford a Facebook ad campaign to whether you should even start the business. CVP analysis is scalable. Tesla used it to justify the Gigafactory: Elon Musk's team computed the cell volume needed to amortize the factory's fixed costs, compared it to Tesla's forecast demand, and used the gap to justify the $5 billion investment. Southwest Airlines uses CVP to price fares: every seat sold above break-even load factor is almost pure profit, which is why Southwest is willing to sell last-minute seats at steep discounts. CVP is not just academic — it is the arithmetic that underlies operational and strategic decisions daily. Its limits are equally important: it assumes linearity, it assumes sales mix stays constant, it assumes you can separate fixed and variable reliably, and it ignores interactions between price and volume. Every CVP user must remember these limits or the tool will mislead them.
key_concepts: Contribution margin per unit: selling price per unit minus variable cost per unit; the dollar amount each unit contributes toward fixed cost and profit
Contribution margin ratio: contribution margin as a percentage of sales; tells you how much of each sales dollar is available to cover fixed costs and add to profit
Break-even in units: fixed costs divided by contribution margin per unit; the volume at which total revenue equals total cost and profit is zero
Break-even in sales dollars: fixed costs divided by contribution margin ratio; used when unit volumes are not meaningful (e.g., service firms or multiproduct businesses)
Target profit (pre-tax): (fixed costs + target pre-tax profit) / contribution margin per unit gives units needed; same formula with contribution margin ratio gives sales dollars needed
Target profit (after-tax): convert target after-tax profit to pre-tax by dividing by (1 - tax rate), then apply the standard target-profit formula
Multiproduct CVP: compute weighted-average contribution margin using a constant sales mix, then use that in place of per-unit CM; sales-mix changes invalidate the result
Margin of safety: actual (or budgeted) sales minus break-even sales; expressed in dollars or as a percentage of actual sales; measures how much sales can drop before losses begin
Operating leverage: contribution margin divided by operating income; measures the percentage change in operating income for a 1% change in sales; high fixed cost structures have high leverage

Assumptions and limits: linearity within relevant range, constant sales mix, constant selling price, cost behavior classifiable into fixed and variable, inventory levels unchanged (production = sales)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Two-part CVP problem with real numbers. Part A: A single-product firm sells widgets at $50 each. Variable cost per unit is $30. Fixed costs are $200,000. Compute CM per unit = $20, CM ratio = 40%, break-even = 10,000 units ($500,000 sales). Target profit of $60,000 pre-tax requires ($200,000 + $60,000) / $20 = 13,000 units. Target profit of $60,000 after-tax at 25% rate requires converting to pre-tax: $60,000 / 0.75 = $80,000, then ($200,000 + $80,000) / $20 = 14,000 units. Margin of safety at 14,000 units: ($700,000 - $500,000) / $700,000 = 28.6%. Operating leverage at 14,000 units: ($20 × 14,000) / ($280,000 - $200,000) = $280,000 / $80,000 = 3.5. If sales increase 10%, operating income rises 35%. Part B: Multiproduct. Firm sells Standard (price $40, VC $24, 60% of units) and Premium (price $80, VC $40, 40% of units). Weighted CM = 0.6 × $16 + 0.4 × $40 = $9.60 + $16 = $25.60. If fixed costs are $256,000, break-even total units = 10,000. At the constant mix: 6,000 Standard and 4,000 Premium. Now discuss: what if the mix shifts to 50/50? Weighted CM changes to $28, break-even drops to 9,143 units — the same fixed costs are absorbed faster when the product mix contains more of the higher-margin product, which is why product-mix management matters as much as cost control.
setup_requirements: CVP graph template (cost/revenue on y-axis, volume on x-axis)
Real company dataset (Tesla Model 3 contribution margin estimates from 10-K and analyst reports)

review.key: Contribution Margin Is What Pays the Fixed Costs
review.value: Every unit above break-even adds its full contribution margin to operating income because fixed costs are already covered; this asymmetry is why growth is so profitable for firms past their break-even point

review.key: Break-Even Is Not a Target, It Is a Floor
review.value: A firm operating at break-even is earning zero profit; the useful managerial question is not 'what is break-even?' but 'how far above break-even are we, and how far can we fall before trouble?'

A high-leverage firm like an airline or semiconductor fab can see operating income move three or four times as fast as sales in both directions; recessions are brutal for high-leverage firms and booms are spectacular

Multiproduct CVP assumes a constant sales mix; in real life the mix shifts with pricing, promotions, and customer preferences, and the break-even changes with it — always re-run CVP when the mix changes materially

class_schedule.topic: Job-Order Costing: Accumulating Costs for Unique Products
learning_objectives: Explain when job-order costing is appropriate and how it differs from process costing
Flow direct materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead through work-in-process, finished goods, and cost of goods sold
Calculate predetermined overhead rates and apply overhead to jobs
Compute, analyze, and dispose of over- or under-applied overhead at period end
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 2 - Job-Order Costing: Calculating Unit Product Costs
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 4 - Job Costing
Drury, Management and Cost Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 4 - Accounting for Direct Costs

lecture_notes.introduction: Job-order costing is the cost system used when each unit or batch produced is distinguishable from others. A custom home, a Hollywood film, a legal engagement, an audit, a hospital patient's treatment, a printing job at Kinko's, and a Boeing 787 are all jobs in this sense. Each one accumulates its own costs: the lumber and fixtures for this specific house, the actors and location days for this specific film, the hours of each specific partner on this specific audit engagement. Job costing is a big deal because it is the only way to know whether a specific customer, project, or client is profitable. Without job costing, Boeing would have no way to know whether the 737 MAX line is making money while the 777 line is losing money; all it would see is a blended average that tells it nothing. Consulting firms live and die by job costing: if McKinsey cannot separate the cost of one engagement from another, it cannot bill hours accurately, it cannot evaluate project managers, and it cannot decide which clients to keep. The mechanics of job costing are straightforward in principle but subtle in practice. Direct materials and direct labor are traced to specific jobs through materials requisitions and time tickets. Manufacturing overhead cannot be traced directly because it is shared across jobs; instead, it is applied to jobs using a predetermined overhead rate based on a cost driver such as direct labor hours or machine hours. The predetermined rate is calculated at the start of the period using estimates, and at period end the applied overhead almost never equals actual overhead — the difference is called over- or under-applied overhead, and how it is disposed of matters for reported product costs and financial statements.
key_concepts: Job-order costing: each job (unit, batch, or project) accumulates its own costs on a job cost sheet; appropriate when products or services are heterogeneous

Process costing: costs are accumulated by department or process and averaged across homogeneous units; appropriate for continuous-flow production such as oil refining, food processing, or commodity manufacturing
Materials requisition form: authorizes release of direct materials from raw materials inventory to a specific job; serves as the tracing document linking material cost to job
Time ticket or labor clock: records the hours each direct laborer spent on each job; the tracing document linking labor cost to job

Predetermined overhead rate: estimated manufacturing overhead divided by estimated allocation base (direct labor hours, machine hours, direct labor cost); calculated at the beginning of the period and used throughout the period to apply overhead

Allocation base (cost driver): the measure used to assign overhead to jobs; should be causally related to overhead consumption; machine hours for automated production, labor hours for labor-intensive work, labor dollars for simple systems
Flow of costs: Raw Materials to Work in Process to Finished Goods to Cost of Goods Sold; direct materials and labor go straight to WIP; overhead is applied to WIP based on cost driver consumption
Over-applied overhead: applied overhead exceeds actual overhead; WIP (and COGS) were charged more than actual — credit balance in overhead control account
Under-applied overhead: applied overhead is less than actual; WIP and COGS were undercharged — debit balance in overhead control account
Disposition of variance: small variances are closed directly to COGS; material variances are prorated across WIP, Finished Goods, and COGS proportionally to their ending balances of applied overhead
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full job-order example for a custom cabinet maker. Month begins with three jobs in WIP: Job 101 (home kitchen, $8,000), Job 102 (office built-ins, $3,000), Job 103 (retail display cases, $1,500). During the month, materials requisitioned: Job 101 $5,000, Job 102 $12,000, Job 103 $6,000. Direct labor: Job 101 40 hours, Job 102 120 hours, Job 103 80 hours at $30/hour. Predetermined overhead rate set at start of year: estimated overhead $600,000, estimated labor hours 25,000, rate = $24/labor hour. Apply overhead: Job 101 40 × $24 = $960; Job 102 120 × $24 = $2,880; Job 103 80 × $24 = $1,920. Total costs: Job 101 = $8,000 + $5,000 + $1,200 + $960 = $15,160; Job 102 = $3,000 + $12,000 + $3,600 + $2,880 = $21,480; Job 103 = $1,500 + $6,000 + $2,400 + $1,920 = $11,820. Jobs 101 and 103 completed and delivered — transfer to Finished Goods then COGS. Job 102 still in WIP. At month-end, actual overhead turned out to be $52,000 but applied overhead was only $48,000 (total hours across all jobs times $24). Under-applied overhead = $4,000. If balances are small enough, close $4,000 debit to COGS. Gross margin on Job 101: if bid price was $20,000, profit = $20,000 - $15,160 = $4,840 or 24.2% gross margin. Without job costing, the cabinet maker could not compute this and would not know whether the bid was priced correctly.
setup_requirements: Materials requisition and time ticket samples
Excel worksheet with flow of costs

review.value: Choose job costing when units or batches are distinguishable and process costing when production is continuous-flow and units are fungible; hybrid operations use operation costing, which mixes features of both

Applying overhead monthly at actual rates would produce absurd variability — a job completed in August would cost more than the same job completed in July just because of seasonal overhead fluctuations; predetermined rates smooth this out
Using machine hours for a hand-assembly shop, or labor dollars for a heavily automated plant, creates systematically wrong unit costs — choose the base that causally drives overhead in your operation

Closing all variance to COGS is simple but writes off inventory-related variances immediately; prorating across WIP, FG, and COGS is more accurate when variances are material and inventory balances are significant

learning_objectives: Identify when process costing is appropriate and describe the flow of costs through processing departments
Compute equivalent units of production using the weighted-average and FIFO methods
Assign costs to units transferred out and to ending work in process
Reconcile total costs to account for using a production cost report
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 4 - Process Costing
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 17 - Process Costing
Kaplan & Atkinson, Advanced Management Accounting (3rd ed.), Ch. 3 - Cost Measurement

lecture_notes.introduction: Process costing is used where production is continuous and every unit is essentially identical. Think of Coca-Cola's bottling plants, ExxonMobil's refineries, Frito-Lay's chip lines, or Intel's wafer fabrication. There is no concept of a unique 'job' for the billionth bottle of Coke — it is just like the 999,999,999th. Job costing would be pointless, slow, and staggeringly expensive. Instead, process costing accumulates costs by department and period and divides by the units produced to get a cost per unit. The subtlety is that at any moment, each department has some units that were completed and transferred out and some units that are still in process — half machined, partially refined, midway through assembly. To compute cost per unit, you need a denominator that reflects the work actually done, which means converting partially completed units to 'equivalent units' of fully completed work. If 10,000 units are 80% complete with respect to conversion costs, that is equivalent to 8,000 fully completed units of conversion work. Get the equivalent units wrong and your cost per unit is wrong, which cascades into wrong inventory values, wrong COGS, and wrong operating income. Two methods dominate: weighted average, which blends the prior period's beginning WIP costs with current costs, and FIFO, which keeps them separate. Weighted average is simpler and more common in practice; FIFO is slightly more accurate and is preferred by textbook theorists. GAAP allows both. Most large process manufacturers use some variant of weighted average because the added complexity of FIFO rarely justifies the marginal accuracy gain.
key_concepts: Process costing: accumulate costs by department (not by job) and divide by equivalent units to get cost per unit; used in continuous-flow, homogeneous production

Processing departments: sequential stages of production, each with its own accumulation of direct materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead; costs transfer forward as units move between departments
Transferred-in costs: costs attached to units moving from an upstream department; treated as a kind of direct material for the downstream department

Equivalent units: the number of completed units that would have required the same amount of work as the actual units (completed plus partially-completed); computed separately for materials and for conversion costs
Conversion costs: direct labor plus manufacturing overhead; often added evenly throughout the process, so a unit 60% complete has consumed 60% of conversion costs
Weighted-average method: blends beginning WIP costs with current period costs; equivalent units = units completed and transferred out + ending WIP × completion percentage

FIFO method: keeps beginning WIP costs separate from current period costs; equivalent units = (beginning WIP × percentage to complete) + units started and completed + (ending WIP × percentage complete)
Cost per equivalent unit: total costs to account for divided by equivalent units; calculated separately for materials and conversion
Production cost report: the main process costing document with four sections — physical flow of units, equivalent units, cost per equivalent unit, cost assignment reconciliation
Normal vs abnormal spoilage: normal spoilage is expected and absorbed into good units; abnormal spoilage is charged immediately as a period loss
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sugar refinery example. Blending Department, June. Beginning WIP: 5,000 tons, 100% complete as to materials, 40% complete as to conversion, costs $40,000 materials + $12,000 conversion. Started in June: 60,000 tons. Completed and transferred: 58,000 tons. Ending WIP: 7,000 tons, 100% materials, 50% conversion. Current period costs: materials $420,000, conversion $198,000. Compute weighted-average equivalent units: materials = 58,000 + 7,000 = 65,000; conversion = 58,000 + 3,500 = 61,500. Cost per equivalent unit: materials = ($40,000 + $420,000) / 65,000 = $7.077; conversion = ($12,000 + $198,000) / 61,500 = $3.415. Assign costs: transferred out 58,000 × ($7.077 + $3.415) = $608,536; ending WIP = 7,000 × $7.077 + 3,500 × $3.415 = $49,539 + $11,953 = $61,492. Reconcile: total cost to account for = $40,000 + $12,000 + $420,000 + $198,000 = $670,000; cost assigned = $608,536 + $61,492 = $670,028 (rounding). Verify the numbers tie out within a few dollars of rounding and walk through what happens if ending WIP had been reported as 90% complete on conversion instead of 50%: equivalent units rise, cost per equivalent unit drops, and $12,000 of cost shifts from transferred-out to ending WIP. Small changes in completion percentage have real valuation consequences.
setup_requirements: Excel workbook with weighted-average and FIFO side-by-side

review.key: Equivalent Units Are the Core Idea
review.value: Partially completed units must be converted to an equivalent number of fully completed units before dividing costs; without this conversion, cost per unit is meaningless

review.key: Materials vs Conversion Are Usually Different
review.value: Materials are often added all at once at the beginning, so ending WIP is 100% complete as to materials; conversion (labor plus overhead) is typically added evenly, so ending WIP is partially complete; computing equivalent units separately reflects this reality

Weighted average blends beginning WIP into current costs and is simpler; FIFO separates them and is slightly more accurate when input prices change rapidly; both are GAAP-acceptable and the choice should match the importance of period-to-period comparability

Nobody measures a vat of molasses to decide whether it is 40% or 50% converted; process accountants estimate completion based on physical observation and engineering judgment, and these estimates directly affect reported costs and inventory

class_schedule.topic: Activity-Based Costing: Tracing Overhead to Products That Actually Consume It
learning_objectives: Explain the conditions under which traditional costing systems distort product costs
Identify activities, cost pools, and cost drivers at each level of the ABC hierarchy
Compute product costs using activity-based costing and compare them with traditional costing results
Evaluate ABC implementation trade-offs including data collection cost and organizational resistance
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 7 - Activity-Based Costing
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 5 - Activity-Based Costing and Activity-Based Management
Kaplan & Anderson, 'Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing,' Harvard Business Review (Nov 2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: For most of the 20th century, manufacturers allocated overhead using a single plant-wide rate, usually based on direct labor hours. This worked acceptably when direct labor was the largest cost and overhead was small. By the 1980s, however, manufacturing had changed. Direct labor had shrunk to 5-15% of total cost in most industries. Overhead — automation, quality systems, IT, scheduling, setups, material handling — had exploded to 40-60%. Allocating 60% of cost based on 10% of cost produced wildly wrong unit costs, and the wrongness was systematic. High-volume simple products were overcharged because their labor hours did not capture their low overhead consumption; low-volume complex products were undercharged because their labor hours missed the disproportionate setup, scheduling, and engineering costs they consumed. Harley-Davidson discovered this in the late 1980s when its traditional cost system said small-volume custom models were highly profitable and high-volume standard models were barely breaking even; activity-based costing revealed the opposite. Hospitals run into the same problem allocating overhead across patients: simple outpatient visits were being charged for complex surgical overhead because the hospital's traditional cost system used a single patient-day rate. ABC, developed by Robin Cooper and Robert Kaplan in the 1980s, addresses this by identifying the activities that consume resources, grouping them into cost pools, and assigning them to products based on the activities each product actually consumes. The price is significant data collection effort, but the insight is often strategy-changing: products that looked profitable are revealed as losers, and customers that looked profitable are revealed as money pits. The most famous ABC lesson is that a handful of customers or products usually generate most of the profit, and another handful is quietly destroying it.
key_concepts: Cost distortion under traditional costing: single-rate systems systematically overcharge high-volume simple products and undercharge low-volume complex products, creating incentives to drop profitable lines and chase unprofitable ones
Activity: a unit of work performed in the organization — purchase order processing, machine setup, quality inspection, order entry; the building block of ABC
Activity cost pool: the total cost assigned to a single activity; all purchase order processing costs, regardless of department, collected into one pool
Cost driver: the measure that causes the activity cost; number of purchase orders, number of setups, number of inspections — chosen to reflect causation

Activity hierarchy: (1) unit-level — performed for each unit produced; (2) batch-level — performed for each batch regardless of size; (3) product-level — performed to support a product line; (4) facility-level — performed to support the plant as a whole
First-stage allocation: assign overhead costs from traditional account categories (plant supervision, utilities, depreciation) to activity cost pools based on resource consumption by each activity
Second-stage allocation: assign activity pool costs to products based on each product's consumption of the activity's driver
Activity-based management (ABM): using ABC data to manage activities — eliminate non-value-adding work, redesign processes, simplify product lines; the operational payoff of the costing insight

Time-driven ABC (Kaplan and Anderson, 2004): simplified variant using capacity cost rates and time equations, reducing the interviewing burden of classical ABC and making the system easier to maintain

ABC implementation costs: data collection is expensive, employees resist being surveyed, IT systems often need modification, and the system must be maintained as processes change — many early ABC projects were abandoned for these reasons
lecture_notes.demonstration: Two-product factory comparing traditional and ABC. Company makes Standard (10,000 units/year, $40 selling price) and Deluxe (1,000 units/year, $120 selling price). Direct materials $15 (S) and $30 (D); direct labor $10 (S) and $20 (D). Total overhead $400,000. Traditional: allocate overhead on labor hours. Standard 10,000 units × 0.5 hour = 5,000 hours; Deluxe 1,000 × 1 hour = 1,000 hours; total 6,000 hours. Plant rate = $400,000 / 6,000 = $66.67 per labor hour. Overhead per unit: S = 0.5 × $66.67 = $33.33; D = 1 × $66.67 = $66.67. Unit cost: S = $15 + $10 + $33.33 = $58.33; D = $30 + $20 + $66.67 = $116.67. Gross profit: S = $40 - $58.33 = -$18.33 per unit, Deluxe $120 - $116.67 = $3.33 per unit. Traditional says Deluxe is profitable, Standard is a disaster. Now run ABC. Identify four activities: machine setups (pool $100,000, driver 200 setups total: S needs 20, D needs 180); machining (pool $120,000, driver 6,000 machine hours: S 4,000, D 2,000); inspection (pool $60,000, driver 500 inspections: S 100, D 400); general operations (pool $120,000, driver labor hours: S 5,000, D 1,000). Activity rates: setups $500/setup, machining $20/hour, inspection $120/inspection, general ops $20/hour. Overhead assigned to Standard: 20 × $500 + 4,000 × $20 + 100 × $120 + 5,000 × $20 = $10,000 + $80,000 + $12,000 + $100,000 = $202,000, or $20.20/unit. Overhead assigned to Deluxe: 180 × $500 + 2,000 × $20 + 400 × $120 + 1,000 × $20 = $90,000 + $40,000 + $48,000 + $20,000 = $198,000, or $198/unit. ABC unit costs: S = $15 + $10 + $20.20 = $45.20; D = $30 + $20 + $198 = $248. Gross profit: S = -$5.20 per unit, D = -$128 per unit. ABC reveals Standard is only modestly unprofitable and can probably be fixed with a small price increase; Deluxe is catastrophically unprofitable and should either be dramatically repriced or discontinued. Traditional costing led management toward exactly the wrong conclusion.
setup_requirements: Comparison template: traditional vs ABC results

review.value: Single-rate overhead allocation produces systematically distorted costs when overhead is large and diverse and when products differ in complexity; ABC is not academic — it can show that products thought to be profitable are losing money
Unit, batch, product, and facility-level costs behave differently with respect to volume; recognizing the level helps you pick the right driver and diagnose where cost distortion comes from

review.key: ABC Often Shifts Cost, Rarely Shifts Cost In Total
review.value: ABC redistributes the same total overhead to different products; the sum of product costs under ABC equals the sum under traditional costing in the short run; the value is in the redistribution, not in finding new money
ABC is conceptually simple but operationally hard; data collection is expensive, sustained commitment is rare, and many organizations abandon it; time-driven ABC exists to reduce this burden

learning_objectives: Explain the purposes of budgeting and the behavioral issues in the budget-setting process
Construct the components of a master budget including sales, production, direct materials, direct labor, overhead, and cash budgets
Prepare a budgeted income statement and balance sheet
Evaluate trade-offs between top-down, bottom-up, and participative budgeting
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 8 - Master Budgeting
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 6 - Master Budget and Responsibility Accounting
Hope & Fraser, Beyond Budgeting (HBS Press, 2003), Ch. 1 - The Trouble with Budgeting

lecture_notes.introduction: Budgeting is how organizations commit the future. The master budget is a comprehensive plan for a period — typically a year — expressed in financial and operational terms. It coordinates activities across departments, communicates management's expectations, sets targets for performance evaluation, and allocates resources. Done well, it aligns dozens or hundreds of managers around a shared plan. Done badly, it produces gaming behavior, padded numbers, sandbagging, and end-of-year spending sprees to protect next year's allocation. Budgeting research since the 1980s has documented that participative budgets — where operating managers help set the targets — produce higher commitment but also more slack (padding) than imposed budgets. Top-down budgets are lean but face rejection and disengagement from the people who must execute them. No process fully escapes these trade-offs. Real budgets involve negotiation and iteration, not pure formula. Mechanically, the master budget starts with the sales forecast, which drives production, which drives materials and labor and overhead, which drives cash flows, which eventually combine into a budgeted income statement and balance sheet. Every number depends on the sales forecast, so if the sales forecast is wrong the budget is wrong. Companies like Intel have been very public about the fact that budgeting for them is more about resource allocation and cash management than about hitting numbers — their sales forecasts move so fast that holding managers to a twelve-month-old target is counterproductive. At the other end, stable-demand firms like Procter & Gamble treat the budget as a firm commitment that managers are expected to deliver, and variance from budget is closely watched. Both philosophies are defensible; what is not defensible is running a budget process without understanding what it does to people.
key_concepts: Master budget: integrated set of budgets covering all phases of operations and all functional areas for a specific period; culminates in budgeted financial statements
Sales budget: foundation budget; projects unit sales and revenues for each period; source of most downstream numbers
Production budget: required production = budgeted sales + desired ending inventory - beginning inventory; smooths production to match sales and maintain inventory targets
Direct materials budget: required materials = production × materials per unit + desired ending materials inventory - beginning materials inventory; purchases × price = materials purchases cost
Direct labor budget: production × labor hours per unit × labor cost per hour; feeds labor cost into cash budget via payroll timing
Manufacturing overhead budget: separates variable overhead (rate × activity) from fixed overhead (committed amounts) and adjusts for non-cash items like depreciation for cash budget
Selling and administrative expense budget: variable components driven by sales, fixed components committed based on strategic decisions
Cash budget: beginning cash + cash receipts - cash disbursements = ending cash; typically prepared monthly or weekly; identifies financing needs
Budgeted income statement and balance sheet: the pro forma financial statements produced from the operating and cash budgets; what the financials will look like if the plan is executed

Behavioral issues: budget slack (padding), sandbagging (low-balling targets), lowballing to ensure bonuses, end-of-year spending to protect next year's allocation; addressed partly through participative budgeting and partly through non-financial performance measures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a first-quarter master budget for a consumer products firm. Sales budget: January 10,000 units, February 12,000, March 15,000 at $25 each. Sales revenue: $250,000, $300,000, $375,000. Production budget with ending inventory target of 20% of next month's sales. December ending inventory (beginning January inventory) = 2,000 units. January production: sales 10,000 + desired end 2,400 (20% of Feb) - beginning 2,000 = 10,400 units. February: 12,000 + 3,000 - 2,400 = 12,600 units. March: 15,000 + 3,200 (20% of estimated April 16,000) - 3,000 = 15,200 units. Direct materials budget with 2 lbs per unit at $3/lb. January: 10,400 × 2 = 20,800 lbs + desired end 10% of Feb 2,520 - beginning 2,080 = 21,240 lbs. Cost = $63,720. Similarly for Feb and March. Labor budget at 0.5 hour per unit at $18/hour. January: 10,400 × 0.5 × $18 = $93,600. Cash budget with 70% of sales collected in month, 30% the following month, and half of materials paid in month, half next. Walk through how a $400,000 beginning cash balance could dip into required short-term borrowing if January's sales collection is front-loaded and February's production ramp-up is cash-hungry. Close with the budgeted income statement: revenue $925,000, variable costs based on production of 38,200 units, gross margin, operating expenses, and tax to get budgeted net income. This is the discipline of integrating the budget into a coherent whole.
setup_requirements: Budgeted income statement and balance sheet templates

review.value: Every operational budget depends on sales; a bad sales forecast propagates through production, materials, labor, overhead, cash, and income; put the most senior judgment into the sales forecast

review.key: Production Budget Smooths or Follows Demand
review.value: The ending inventory policy determines whether the production plan follows sales (chase strategy) or maintains a level production line (level strategy); each has trade-offs in inventory cost and capacity utilization

A profitable firm can still run out of cash during the year because of timing mismatches between inflows and outflows; cash budgeting identifies these gaps in advance and allows financing to be arranged cheaply

The target people are measured against changes how they act; sandbagging, padding, and end-of-year spending are all predictable responses to budget-as-contract; design the process with behavioral effects in mind

class_schedule.topic: Standard Costing and Variance Analysis: Materials and Labor Variances
learning_objectives: Distinguish standard costs from budgeted costs and explain the role of standards in cost control
Compute the direct materials price variance and direct materials quantity (usage) variance
Compute the direct labor rate variance and direct labor efficiency variance
Interpret variances and identify plausible root causes and responsibility
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 9 - Flexible Budgets, Standard Costs, and Variance Analysis
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 7 - Flexible Budgets, Direct-Cost Variances, and Management Control
Drury, Management and Cost Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 18 - Standard Costing and Variance Analysis 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Standard costing turns the cost system into a control system. Rather than just recording what costs actually were, standard costing compares actual costs to what they should have been at the actual output level and breaks the difference into price variances (did we pay the right amount per unit of input?) and quantity variances (did we use the right amount of input per unit of output?). These variances localize problems and assign responsibility: a materials price variance points to purchasing; a materials quantity variance points to production; a labor rate variance points to HR and scheduling; a labor efficiency variance points to supervision and training. Standard costing was developed in the early 20th century by industrial engineers like Frederick Taylor who believed manufacturing could be optimized the way engineering problems could be optimized — establish the one best way to produce a unit, measure performance against it, and investigate deviations. The method became the backbone of manufacturing cost control for decades and is still the most widely used cost control technique in the United States. Toyota famously rejected classical standard costing on the grounds that it encouraged local optimization at the expense of system performance, but Toyota also built elaborate physical cost controls that classical standard costing was trying to capture. In practice, standard costing remains widespread because it is reasonably cheap, produces actionable signals, and fits the pattern of how most manufacturing organizations are structured. The key is to set standards tight enough to motivate improvement but not so tight that they produce constant unfavorable variances and demoralized workers. Practical standards reflect efficient operation under normal conditions; ideal standards assume perfect efficiency and generally produce more friction than value.
key_concepts: Standard cost: a carefully predetermined cost per unit of output, broken into standard price per input × standard quantity of input per unit of output, for each cost category (materials, labor, overhead)

Ideal vs practical standards: ideal standards assume perfect efficiency with no downtime, no spoilage, no idle time; practical standards allow for normal inefficiencies and are used in most real systems
Static budget: budget based on planned activity level; does not flex when actual volume differs from plan; inappropriate for performance evaluation when volume varies
Flexible budget: budget adjusted to the actual activity level; essential for variance analysis because it separates volume effects from efficiency effects

Direct materials price variance: (Actual price - Standard price) × Actual quantity purchased; measures the effect of paying a different price than standard; computed at purchase (so it is recorded before use)

Direct materials quantity (usage) variance: (Actual quantity used - Standard quantity allowed for actual output) × Standard price; measures the effect of using more or less material than the standard allows for actual production
Direct labor rate variance: (Actual rate - Standard rate) × Actual hours worked; measures wage rate effects, driven by overtime, mix of skill levels, union settlements
Direct labor efficiency variance: (Actual hours - Standard hours allowed for actual output) × Standard rate; measures whether workers produced the expected output per hour
Management by exception: investigate significant variances, ignore small ones; threshold typically set at 5% or 10% of the standard or a fixed dollar amount

Interdependence of variances: a favorable price variance on cheaper materials may produce an unfavorable quantity variance when those cheaper materials have more waste; always look at the whole picture before assigning blame
lecture_notes.demonstration: A furniture manufacturer has a standard for a dining chair: 8 board-feet of oak at $6 per board-foot (standard materials $48) and 2 hours of labor at $20/hour (standard labor $40). In June, the company made 1,000 chairs. Actual materials: purchased and used 8,400 board-feet at $5.80/ft. Actual labor: 1,950 hours at $21.50/hour. Calculate materials variances. Price variance = ($5.80 - $6) × 8,400 = $(1,680) favorable — purchased oak $0.20 below standard. Quantity variance = (8,400 - 8,000) × $6 = $2,400 unfavorable — used 400 board-feet more than the standard of 8,000 allowed for 1,000 chairs. Net materials variance = $720 unfavorable. Labor variances. Rate = ($21.50 - $20) × 1,950 = $2,925 unfavorable — paid $1.50/hour more than standard, likely overtime or premium shift. Efficiency = (1,950 - 2,000) × $20 = $(1,000) favorable — used 50 fewer hours than the 2,000 standard, meaning the workforce was faster. Net labor variance = $1,925 unfavorable. Investigation: Why did materials use exceed standard while labor was faster? Plausible hypothesis: the purchasing manager bought a cheaper lower-grade oak with more knots and warp ($0.20/ft saving) that generated more waste (400 extra board-feet). Meanwhile, the production team worked overtime at premium rates to push through the more difficult material. The favorable price variance triggered a chain of unfavorable consequences that exceeded the savings. This is classic interdependence of variances and illustrates why variance investigation cannot stop at the surface — you must trace root causes.
setup_requirements: Excel with formulas for all four variances

review.key: Standard Costs Are Targets, Not Historical Averages
review.value: A standard is what cost should be, set by engineering study or management judgment, not a rolling average of historical cost; using historical averages locks in past inefficiencies

review.key: Price Variance at Purchase, Quantity at Use
review.value: The materials price variance is recorded when materials are purchased because responsibility lies with the buyer at that moment; the quantity variance is recorded when materials are used because responsibility lies with production

review.key: Flexible Budget Separates Volume from Efficiency
review.value: Comparing actual to static budget mixes volume differences with efficiency differences; flexible budget isolates efficiency by adjusting the static budget to the actual volume produced
A favorable variance in one area may cause an unfavorable variance in another; always investigate root causes across variances before assigning responsibility or changing standards

class_schedule.topic: Overhead Variances and the Four-Variance Model
learning_objectives: Compute variable overhead spending and efficiency variances
Compute fixed overhead budget and volume variances
Explain the four-variance model and the two-variance alternative
Interpret the fixed overhead volume variance with caution and understand its limited decision relevance
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 9 - Flexible Budgets, Standard Costs, and Variance Analysis (overhead section)
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 8 - Flexible Budgets, Overhead Cost Variances, and Management Control
Drury, Management and Cost Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 19 - Standard Costing and Variance Analysis 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Overhead variance analysis is the most technically demanding part of standard costing. Materials and labor behave as pure variable costs in the standard cost system — the standard is a rate per unit of input, the variance flows through just two dimensions (price and quantity), and the interpretation is relatively clean. Overhead is neither purely variable nor purely fixed. Variable overhead (indirect materials, indirect labor, utilities) scales with activity. Fixed overhead (supervision, depreciation, property tax) is committed independent of activity. The two types need different variance frameworks. Variable overhead splits into a spending variance (were per-hour rates higher or lower than standard?) and an efficiency variance (did we use more or fewer hours than standard, which drives more or less variable overhead?). Fixed overhead splits into a budget variance (did we spend more or less than budget on fixed overhead?) and a volume variance (did we produce at the expected volume for which fixed overhead was absorbed?). The volume variance is the most misunderstood number in managerial accounting. It is an artifact of GAAP — fixed overhead must be allocated to units for absorption costing — and represents the difference between the fixed overhead applied to units and the total fixed overhead in the budget. It has no cash consequences, it is not controllable by operating managers, and it can punish efficient management for producing at a lower volume than planned even when that is the correct economic decision. Textbook presentations sometimes treat the volume variance as a performance measure. It is not, except in very unusual circumstances. A more useful lens is to separate the true controllable variances (variable overhead spending, variable overhead efficiency, fixed overhead budget) from the allocation artifact (volume variance) and focus management attention on the first three.
key_concepts: Variable overhead spending variance: (Actual VOH rate - Standard VOH rate) × Actual hours; similar to materials price variance but applied to variable overhead per hour

Variable overhead efficiency variance: (Actual hours - Standard hours allowed) × Standard VOH rate; a derivative of the labor efficiency variance — if labor is inefficient, variable overhead is inefficient too
Fixed overhead budget variance: Actual fixed overhead - Budgeted fixed overhead; measures whether the fixed overhead budget was hit regardless of volume produced

Fixed overhead volume variance: Budgeted fixed overhead - Fixed overhead applied to production (Standard hours allowed × Fixed OH rate); arises from producing at a volume different from the denominator level used to compute the fixed OH rate

Denominator level: the volume assumption used to compute the predetermined fixed overhead rate; choosing practical capacity vs normal activity vs master budget volume changes the size of the volume variance
Predetermined fixed OH rate: Budgeted fixed OH / Denominator activity level; applied to production at standard hours allowed × rate
Four-variance model: spending, efficiency, budget, volume; most detailed model and used for internal control
Three-variance model: combines variable spending and fixed budget into a 'spending' variance, plus efficiency and volume
Two-variance model: controllable (variable spending + efficiency + fixed budget) vs volume; simplest form

Volume variance interpretation: not a performance measure of efficiency; merely reflects the arithmetic of unit absorption; unfavorable volume variance means production fell short of denominator assumption, which may be desirable or undesirable depending on circumstances
lecture_notes.demonstration: Same furniture manufacturer from the previous session. Standard variable OH is $8 per direct labor hour. Standard fixed OH is $15 per labor hour, based on 2,000 standard hours at denominator level of 1,000 chairs/month × 2 hours = 2,000 hours, and a budgeted fixed OH of $30,000. June actual fixed OH was $31,500. June actual variable OH was $16,300. June actual direct labor hours were 1,950. Standard hours allowed for 1,000 chairs = 2,000 hours. Variable overhead spending variance: (Actual VOH rate - Standard VOH rate) × Actual hours = (($16,300/1,950) - $8) × 1,950 = ($8.359 - $8) × 1,950 = $700 unfavorable. Variable overhead efficiency variance: (1,950 - 2,000) × $8 = ($400) favorable. Net variable OH = $300 unfavorable. Fixed overhead budget variance: $31,500 - $30,000 = $1,500 unfavorable. Fixed overhead volume variance: $30,000 budgeted - ($15 × 2,000 standard hours allowed) = $30,000 - $30,000 = $0. At exactly the denominator level, the volume variance is zero. Now imagine instead June production was 900 chairs with 1,800 standard hours allowed. Volume variance: $30,000 - ($15 × 1,800) = $30,000 - $27,000 = $3,000 unfavorable — simply because fewer chairs absorbed the fixed overhead. There was no efficiency failure; the company just produced 10% less volume. Discussion: in the second scenario, is the $3,000 unfavorable volume variance the plant manager's fault? Only if the plant manager controls volume. If demand was lower and sales ordered fewer chairs, the volume shortfall is a sales issue, not a plant issue, and punishing the plant manager for it is wrong. This is the subtlety that makes overhead variances tricky and that new managers get backwards.
setup_requirements: Flexible budget comparison with static budget
Real manufacturing budget with actual-versus-budget fixed and variable components

review.key: Variable vs Fixed Need Different Variances
review.value: Variable OH gets a spending variance and an efficiency variance paralleling labor; fixed OH gets a budget variance (controllable) and a volume variance (arithmetic artifact)

review.key: The Volume Variance Is Not an Efficiency Measure
review.value: It simply tells you the difference between applied fixed overhead and budgeted fixed overhead, which depends on volume produced and the denominator level chosen; do not interpret it as operational performance

review.key: Variable OH Efficiency Is Parasitic on Labor
review.value: When variable OH is applied on labor hours, the variable OH efficiency variance tracks exactly with labor efficiency; the two numbers cannot move independently

Practical capacity produces a bigger unfavorable volume variance during slow periods and a smaller favorable one during busy periods; normal activity smooths the variance over time; choose consciously

class_schedule.topic: Relevant Costs for Short-Run Decisions: Make-or-Buy, Special Orders, and Product-Line Decisions
learning_objectives: Distinguish relevant from irrelevant costs in short-run operating decisions
Analyze a make-or-buy decision considering avoidable costs, opportunity costs, and qualitative factors
Evaluate a special order using incremental analysis and appropriate capacity conditions
Evaluate keep-or-drop decisions for product lines and analyze sell-or-process-further decisions for joint products
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 12 - Differential Analysis: The Key to Decision Making
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 11 - Decision Making and Relevant Information
Drury, Management and Cost Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 9 - Measuring Relevant Costs and Revenues for Decision-Making

lecture_notes.introduction: Relevant costing is the art of ignoring the right things. In every operating decision, some costs will differ between alternatives and some will not. The ones that differ are relevant; the ones that do not are irrelevant, even if they are large. A cost that is sunk — already incurred and unrecoverable — is irrelevant to any future decision, no matter how painful the sunk cost was. A cost that is committed but unavoidable across all alternatives is irrelevant because it will be incurred either way. An opportunity cost — the profit given up by not choosing the next-best alternative — is relevant even though it never appears in the accounting records. The mind resists these rules. Nobody wants to hear that the $10 million spent on a failed project does not matter anymore; they want that money counted against future alternatives as a reason to keep going. The sunk cost fallacy is one of the most robustly demonstrated errors in business decision making, and it is the single most common way that managers make relevant-cost mistakes. Real cases illustrate how dangerous this can be. Eastern Airlines in the 1980s kept underperforming routes open because it felt it had 'invested' in the slots and the crew bases, even though the economics of continuing were worse than the economics of dropping them. Dell's famous decision in the 2000s to outsource motherboard production to Asus was framed as a make-or-buy decision, but Dell's relevant-cost analysis missed the opportunity cost — it was giving Asus the platform to become a future competitor, which is exactly what happened. Good relevant-cost analysis is disciplined about what it counts and what it ignores, and it is expansive about opportunity costs and qualitative factors even when they cannot be precisely measured.
key_concepts: Relevant cost: a cost that differs between alternatives in a specific decision context; also called differential cost or incremental cost
Sunk cost: a cost already incurred that cannot be recovered; always irrelevant to future decisions; the cost of an old machine is sunk and irrelevant to whether to replace it
Opportunity cost: the benefit foregone by choosing one alternative over another; relevant but not in the accounting records; must be estimated and included in analysis
Avoidable cost: a cost that will be eliminated if a particular alternative is chosen; the avoidable portion of fixed costs is especially important in keep-or-drop decisions
Unavoidable cost: a committed cost that will continue regardless of the decision; irrelevant; the salary of the CEO, the depreciation of the headquarters

Make-or-buy analysis: compare the cost of producing internally (avoidable costs only, plus opportunity cost of capacity) with the cost of buying externally; qualitative factors include quality control, supplier reliability, and strategic capability
Special order analysis: evaluate the incremental revenue versus incremental cost of a one-time order; appropriate when capacity is idle and the order does not disturb regular pricing or markets
Capacity conditions: idle capacity makes special orders easy (only variable costs are relevant); full capacity introduces opportunity cost of displaced regular business
Keep-or-drop analysis: compute the contribution margin lost plus the fixed costs avoided; drop only if fixed costs avoided exceed contribution margin given up
Sell-or-process-further decisions: joint costs of common processing are irrelevant (sunk); compare incremental revenue of further processing with incremental cost of further processing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Three mini-cases. Case 1: Make-or-buy. A company makes component Y internally with direct materials $8, direct labor $12, variable OH $5, allocated fixed OH $10 = total $35. A supplier offers Y for $28. Naive analysis says buy because $28 < $35. Relevant analysis: if the fixed OH is unavoidable (still incurred if component is purchased), only the avoidable portion counts. Suppose $7 of the $10 fixed OH is unavoidable and $3 can be avoided (eliminate supervisor position). Relevant cost to make = $8 + $12 + $5 + $3 = $28. At exactly $28 the company is indifferent on cost; qualitative factors decide. If the capacity freed up could earn $4 of contribution margin elsewhere, the opportunity cost of making shifts the decision: relevant cost to make becomes $28 + $4 = $32, so buying at $28 saves $4 per unit. Case 2: Special order. Firm has capacity to produce 100,000 units per year. Current production 80,000 units at $15 price, variable cost $9, fixed cost $3, total cost $12. A customer offers to buy 15,000 units at $10 per unit. Regular market at $15 is unaffected. Incremental revenue $150,000; incremental variable cost $135,000; incremental fixed cost $0 (idle capacity). Incremental profit $15,000 — accept. But if capacity is tight and accepting the special order displaces 5,000 units of regular sales at $15 - $9 = $6 CM, opportunity cost $30,000; incremental profit becomes $15,000 - $30,000 = -$15,000 — reject. Capacity is the key variable. Case 3: Product-line drop. A product contributes $100,000 gross margin, with $60,000 variable costs and $30,000 allocated fixed costs. Dropping it would save $10,000 of traceable fixed costs (only a third of the allocated amount — the other two-thirds cover rent and plant supervision that are unavoidable). Analysis: drop decision saves $10,000 but loses contribution margin of $40,000 (revenue $100,000 - variable $60,000). Net effect: ($40,000) — drop would decrease operating income by $30,000, not improve it. Keep the product. The trap is thinking the full $30,000 of allocated fixed cost would be avoided; only the traceable portion is.
setup_requirements: Relevant cost worksheet separating avoidable and unavoidable
Case handouts: Dell/Asus (1996-2010), Eastern Airlines, joint product processing

review.value: No matter how painful, no matter how large, sunk costs do not affect future decisions; only future differential cash flows matter; catching yourself arguing from sunk costs is a warning sign

Allocated fixed costs often look like they will go away when a product is dropped, but most will not — they are committed at the plant or corporate level; always separate traceable/avoidable fixed costs from allocated fixed costs
Idle capacity makes special orders easy; full capacity introduces displacement and opportunity cost; the relevant cost of a special order is fundamentally different in the two cases
Make-or-buy decisions involve quality, dependence, competitive dynamics, and learning effects that may override the pure-cost calculation; Dell/Asus is the cautionary example

class_schedule.topic: Capital Budgeting: NPV, IRR, Payback, and Decision Rules
learning_objectives: Apply the time value of money to cash flows occurring at different points in time
Compute net present value, internal rate of return, payback period, and profitability index for a capital project
Select among competing projects using appropriate decision rules and constraints
Perform sensitivity analysis on key assumptions and interpret the results
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 13 - Capital Budgeting Decisions
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 21 - Capital Budgeting and Cost Analysis
Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 5 - Net Present Value and Other Investment Criteria

lecture_notes.introduction: Capital budgeting decisions commit large amounts of cash to projects that span years or decades, and the stakes are often existential. Tesla's Gigafactory decision was a $5 billion capital budgeting problem. Apple's Apple Park headquarters was $5 billion. Intel's Arizona fabs are running $20 billion each. Boeing's 787 Dreamliner program was $20 billion and produced losses for a decade. Every one of these decisions was made using some version of discounted cash flow analysis — projecting future cash flows, discounting them to present value, comparing to the initial investment, and deciding. The core insight of DCF is that a dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future because today's dollar can be invested and grow. The discount rate captures the required return on investment, which reflects the riskiness of the project and the opportunity cost of capital. Net present value, the preferred metric, sums all discounted cash flows and accepts projects with positive NPV. Internal rate of return, the rate that makes NPV zero, is conceptually appealing but has well-known pitfalls — multiple IRRs for nonconventional cash flows, wrong rankings for mutually exclusive projects, and no account of project scale. Payback period measures how long it takes to recover the initial investment and is rough and ready, useful mainly as a liquidity screen rather than a profitability measure. Profitability index scales NPV by investment and helps rank projects when capital is rationed. In the real world, companies use multiple metrics because each one catches problems the others miss, and because good capital budgeting is as much about challenging forecasts as about calculating present values. The most famous managerial failures in capital budgeting are rarely NPV arithmetic errors; they are cases where unrealistic forecasts or ignored risks produced a seemingly positive NPV for a project that was a disaster. Enron's broadband investments, Time Warner's AOL merger, and Hewlett-Packard's Autonomy acquisition all passed some version of DCF analysis before turning out to be value-destroying. Discipline around assumptions is what separates capital budgeting from wishful thinking.
key_concepts: Time value of money: cash flows occurring at different times cannot be compared directly and must be adjusted to a common point in time using discounting (future to present) or compounding (present to future)
Present value: PV = FV / (1+r)^n, where r is the discount rate and n is the number of periods; sums to NPV when applied to a stream of cash flows
Discount rate: the minimum acceptable rate of return; reflects risk of the project and opportunity cost of capital; typically the firm's weighted-average cost of capital for average-risk projects
Net present value (NPV): sum of discounted cash flows minus initial investment; accept projects with NPV > 0; measures expected wealth creation

Internal rate of return (IRR): the discount rate that produces NPV = 0; accept if IRR > required rate; pitfalls include nonconventional cash flow streams with multiple IRRs and ranking conflicts with NPV

Payback period: years to recover the initial investment from undiscounted cash flows; simple but ignores time value and post-payback cash flows; used as a liquidity screen, not a profitability measure
Discounted payback period: payback using discounted cash flows; addresses time value but still ignores post-payback flows
Profitability index: PV of future cash flows divided by initial investment; accept if PI > 1; useful for ranking under capital rationing
Sensitivity analysis: test how NPV changes when key inputs (revenue, cost, discount rate, salvage value) vary individually; identifies the inputs that matter most
Scenario analysis: build discrete scenarios (best case, base case, worst case) with internally consistent assumption bundles; produces a distribution of NPV outcomes rather than a point estimate
lecture_notes.demonstration: A manufacturing firm is considering a $500,000 investment in new equipment that will produce net cash inflows of $150,000 per year for 5 years with no salvage value. Required rate of return is 10%. Payback: $500,000 / $150,000 = 3.33 years, fine if the cutoff is 4 years. NPV calculation at 10%: PV of $150,000 annuity for 5 years at 10% = $150,000 × 3.7908 = $568,620. NPV = $568,620 - $500,000 = $68,620. Positive — accept. IRR: find the rate that makes NPV = 0. Using trial or Excel's IRR function: approximately 15.24%. Above 10% — accept. Profitability index: $568,620 / $500,000 = 1.137. Above 1 — accept. All four metrics agree for this clean project. Now complicate it. Year 3 cash flow drops to $80,000 because of an anticipated competitor entry; years 1, 2, 4, 5 unchanged at $150,000. Recompute NPV: PV of Year 1 $150,000/1.10 = $136,364; Year 2 $150,000/1.21 = $123,967; Year 3 $80,000/1.331 = $60,105; Year 4 $150,000/1.4641 = $102,452; Year 5 $150,000/1.61051 = $93,138. Sum = $516,026. NPV = $516,026 - $500,000 = $16,026. Still positive but barely. Run a sensitivity analysis: if the discount rate rises to 12%, NPV drops to -$7,600 and the project should be rejected. If cash flows fall 10% across the board, NPV drops to -$34,000. The project is highly sensitive to both the discount rate and the cash flow forecast, and a responsible manager would want to understand the basis for these forecasts deeply before committing. This is the essence of capital budgeting: the arithmetic is the easy part, but the judgment on assumptions is where value is made or destroyed.
setup_requirements: Excel with NPV, IRR, and payback formulas
Sensitivity analysis template with data tables
Real capital project case (Tesla Gigafactory, Intel fab, or Boeing 787)

review.key: NPV Is the Theoretically Correct Criterion
review.value: NPV measures the wealth created by a project in today's dollars and sums correctly across all alternatives; use it as the primary decision rule and use IRR and payback as supplemental information

Non-conventional cash flows produce multiple IRRs; mutually exclusive projects with different scales or timing can be ranked differently by IRR than by NPV; always check NPV when IRR rankings are ambiguous

review.key: Payback Is a Liquidity Screen, Not a Profitability Measure
review.value: Payback ignores time value and post-payback cash flows; it answers 'when do I get my money back' but not 'should I invest'; use it in combination with NPV, never instead of it

DCF arithmetic is mechanical; the revenue growth, cost reduction, and discount rate assumptions are where errors hide; sensitivity and scenario analysis stress-test the assumptions and reveal when a project is riskier than the point estimate suggests

class_schedule.topic: Responsibility Accounting, Segment Reporting, and Transfer Pricing
learning_objectives: Distinguish cost centers, profit centers, and investment centers and explain appropriate performance measures for each
Prepare segmented income statements using the contribution format and distinguish traceable from common fixed costs
Compute return on investment (ROI) and residual income and discuss the incentive effects of each metric
Evaluate transfer pricing methods (market-based, cost-based, negotiated) and their effects on division and firm-level decisions
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 11 - Performance Measurement in Decentralized Organizations
Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), Ch. 22 - Management Control Systems, Transfer Pricing, and Multinational Considerations
Anthony & Govindarajan, Management Control Systems (12th ed.), Ch. 6 - Transfer Pricing

lecture_notes.introduction: Large organizations decentralize because no central office can make every operational decision well. Divisions, regions, and business units are delegated authority to run their piece of the business, and they are evaluated on their performance. The problem is getting the performance measures right. A division measured on cost control will cut costs even when the cuts hurt revenue. A division measured on revenue growth will chase revenue even when the deals lose money. A division measured on ROI will reject projects that earn above the cost of capital but below the division's current ROI, because accepting them drags the division's average down. Every performance measure is a double-edged sword, and the history of corporate management is littered with the unintended consequences of metrics that seemed reasonable in the boardroom and turned out to drive perverse behavior in the field. General Electric under Jack Welch ran a famously intense responsibility accounting system that produced excellent quarterly results and, over time, questionable long-term investment in research and infrastructure. Amazon runs on a web of responsibility centers whose metrics are tailored to each unit's mission — AWS is a profit center, logistics is partly a cost center, and retail marketing is measured on contribution margin, not gross margin. The mechanics of responsibility accounting include segment reporting (contribution-format statements that separate traceable costs from common costs), return on investment (net operating income divided by average operating assets), and residual income (operating income minus a capital charge based on required return). Transfer pricing — the internal price charged when one division sells to another — is the most politically charged topic because it directly affects each division's measured profit, and the economically optimal transfer price is often rejected by division managers who prefer to maximize their own results. Multinationals face an additional layer of complexity because transfer prices between countries affect tax liabilities in each jurisdiction, and tax authorities rigorously audit these prices.
