key_concepts: Responsibility center: an organizational unit whose manager is held accountable for specific financial outcomes; design choice is cost center, revenue center, profit center, or investment center
Cost center: manager controls costs but not revenues; evaluated on cost performance vs budget; examples include manufacturing plants and corporate functions
Profit center: manager controls both revenues and costs; evaluated on operating income; examples include retail stores and division of a multi-product firm
Investment center: manager controls revenues, costs, and investment in operating assets; evaluated on ROI or residual income; examples include autonomous business units

Segment reporting: contribution-format income statement showing revenues, variable costs, contribution margin, traceable fixed costs (avoidable if segment were eliminated), segment margin, and allocation of common fixed costs
Traceable fixed costs: costs that arise specifically because of a segment and would disappear if the segment disappeared; properly charged to the segment
Common fixed costs: costs shared across segments that would continue even if one segment were eliminated; should not be allocated to segments for decision making but may be allocated for reporting

Return on investment (ROI): net operating income / average operating assets; can be decomposed into margin × turnover using the DuPont identity; widely used but encourages rejecting projects that earn above cost of capital but below current ROI

Residual income: operating income minus (required rate × operating assets); accepts all projects earning above the required rate; preferred by most academics over ROI but harder to explain to managers

Transfer pricing: market-based (use external market price when available; theoretically optimal), cost-based (variable cost or full cost; simple but creates incentive problems), negotiated (division managers agree on a price; preserves autonomy but consumes time and can produce suboptimal outcomes)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Example firm with two divisions. Division A makes a component that is sold externally for $50 per unit. Division B uses the component in a product that sells for $120. Variable cost in Division A is $30; variable cost to finish in Division B is $40. Fixed costs in each division: A $200,000, B $300,000. Sales: A sells 10,000 units externally and transfers 5,000 units internally. B sells 5,000 finished products. Market-based transfer price = $50. A's revenue: 10,000 × $50 + 5,000 × $50 = $750,000; variable costs 15,000 × $30 = $450,000; contribution $300,000; fixed $200,000; segment margin $100,000. B's revenue: 5,000 × $120 = $600,000; variable: transfer 5,000 × $50 + finishing 5,000 × $40 = $250,000 + $200,000 = $450,000; contribution $150,000; fixed $300,000; segment margin -$150,000. Firm total: $100,000 + -$150,000 = -$50,000. Now try variable-cost transfer price of $30. A's internal revenue drops to 5,000 × $30 = $150,000, reducing A's segment margin by $100,000 to $0. B's variable cost drops to 5,000 × $30 + 5,000 × $40 = $350,000, increasing contribution to $250,000 and segment margin to -$50,000. Firm total: $0 + -$50,000 = -$50,000. Same firm-level result (as expected — transfer price is internal) but very different division-level results. Division A managers will hate variable-cost transfer pricing; Division B will love it. The firm-level decision about whether to continue B is unchanged: it loses money at either transfer price, and the question is whether B's operations can be fixed. Discuss how ROI and residual income would affect the decision if A is asked to take on a new project at 12% return when A's current ROI is 18% — A manager rejects because it drags down ROI, but the firm's cost of capital is 10% and accepting the project adds value (residual income is positive). This is the classic disadvantage of ROI and why some firms have switched to residual income or economic value added (EVA).
setup_requirements: Case: Weyerhaeuser or General Electric decentralization

review.value: A manager should only be evaluated on items under her control; holding a plant manager accountable for sales volume she cannot influence produces cynicism and gaming
A division with high ROI will reject projects earning above cost of capital but below current ROI because they dilute the measure; residual income avoids this by focusing on dollar value created

review.key: Common Fixed Costs Should Not Be Allocated for Decisions
review.value: Allocating common costs to segments makes segment margin look worse than it is and can lead to incorrect drop decisions; keep traceable and common costs separate

review.key: Transfer Prices Change Division Results, Not Firm Results
review.value: The firm's total income is the same regardless of the internal transfer price; transfer prices are a zero-sum redistribution between divisions, so they should be set to motivate efficient behavior, not to settle political disputes

class_schedule.topic: Balanced Scorecard and Strategic Performance Measurement
learning_objectives: Explain the four perspectives of the balanced scorecard: financial, customer, internal process, and learning and growth
Construct a strategy map linking cause-and-effect relationships across perspectives
Identify lagging and leading indicators and explain the role of each
Critique balanced scorecard implementations and common failure modes
readings: Kaplan & Norton, 'The Balanced Scorecard — Measures that Drive Performance,' Harvard Business Review (Jan-Feb 1992)
Kaplan & Norton, The Strategy-Focused Organization (HBS Press, 2001), Ch. 1-3
Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 11 (balanced scorecard section)

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial measures alone are lagging indicators — they tell you what already happened, after it is too late to change course. A company looking only at quarterly earnings has no visibility into the customer satisfaction declines, process quality failures, or employee disengagement that will drive next year's earnings. Robert Kaplan and David Norton proposed the balanced scorecard in 1992 as a framework for combining financial measures with non-financial measures to create a forward-looking performance system. The scorecard organizes measures into four perspectives. Financial perspective: how do we look to shareholders? Revenue, operating income, ROI, economic value added. Customer perspective: how do customers see us? Customer satisfaction, retention, market share, on-time delivery. Internal process perspective: at what must we excel? Manufacturing cycle time, defect rates, innovation speed. Learning and growth perspective: can we continue to improve and create value? Employee engagement, training investment, information system capabilities. The insight is that non-financial measures often lead financial measures. Declining customer satisfaction this year shows up as declining sales next year. Rising defect rates this quarter show up as warranty costs and customer churn a quarter or two later. Leading indicators give management the chance to act before the financial consequences hit. Kaplan and Norton expanded the scorecard into a strategy map, which shows cause-and-effect relationships among the four perspectives. Learning and growth enables internal process improvements, which enable customer value delivery, which enables financial results. Drawn well, a strategy map is a hypothesis about how your strategy creates value, and measuring along the way tests the hypothesis. Common failure modes include picking too many measures (20+ dilutes focus), picking measures that are easy to collect rather than strategic, never revising the scorecard as strategy changes, and treating it as a reporting exercise rather than a management tool. Mobil North America famously used the balanced scorecard to turn around its downstream business in the 1990s. Hilton used it to coordinate hundreds of properties around a shared strategy. But many scorecard implementations have failed quietly, and academic research shows mixed results overall. The tool works when leadership uses it to manage; it does not work when it is delegated to finance and treated as a report.
key_concepts: Balanced scorecard: performance measurement framework combining financial and non-financial measures across four perspectives to give a balanced view of organizational performance
Financial perspective: shareholder view; includes revenue, operating income, ROI, EVA, growth measures; the ultimate outcome of good strategy execution
Customer perspective: customer view; satisfaction, retention, acquisition, profitability, market share; bridges financial results and operational activities
Internal process perspective: operational excellence view; cycle time, quality, cost, innovation, supply chain responsiveness; the activities that deliver customer value
Learning and growth perspective: organizational capability view; employee skills, information systems, organizational alignment; the foundation that enables process improvement
Lagging indicator: measures past performance; financial results, customer satisfaction survey results; diagnostic but not predictive
Leading indicator: predicts future performance; pipeline value, defect trends, employee engagement; provides early warning and opportunity for intervention
Strategy map: visual representation of cause-and-effect relationships among scorecard measures across the four perspectives; makes strategy testable and communicable
Cascading the scorecard: pushing measures down from corporate to business unit to department to individual; aligns personal goals with strategic goals

Common failure modes: too many measures, measures not linked to strategy, no revision as strategy changes, treating the scorecard as a report rather than a management tool, no integration with compensation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a balanced scorecard for a mid-sized retail bank focused on growing its small business segment. Financial perspective: SMB revenue growth 15%/year, SMB segment contribution $25M/year, cost-to-income ratio below 55%. Customer perspective: SMB net promoter score above 50, new SMB customer acquisition 500/quarter, share of small business banking in home metropolitan area 18%. Internal process perspective: SMB loan decisioning time below 5 business days, branch satisfaction with SMB product suite 85%+, cross-sell products per SMB client above 3. Learning and growth perspective: SMB relationship manager training hours 40/year, SMB expertise certification 90% of relationship managers, voice-of-employee index above 4.0/5.0. Strategy map hypothesis: invest in training SMB relationship managers, which produces faster and more accurate lending decisions, which produces higher customer satisfaction and retention, which produces higher SMB revenue and contribution. Test the hypothesis by tracking correlations: does training hours correlate with decisioning time six months later? Does decisioning time correlate with NPS three months later? Does NPS correlate with retention and revenue? If any link fails, the strategy is wrong or the measures are wrong. Now discuss what goes wrong. A common failure: the bank rolls out the scorecard but only the CEO and CFO pay attention to it; branch managers are still incentivized on short-term deposit growth; relationship manager bonuses are based on loan volume with no quality check. The scorecard is aspirational but the compensation system is unchanged, and behavior follows compensation. Fix: align the compensation and performance review system with the scorecard measures. This alignment is the single largest difference between scorecards that work and scorecards that gather dust.
setup_requirements: Balanced scorecard template for the four perspectives
Case: Mobil North America, Hilton, or Southwest Airlines scorecard

review.value: Earnings, revenue, and cash are the results of past decisions; by the time they decline, the damage is done; managing only on financial measures is driving by looking in the rear-view mirror
Non-financial measures often lead financial results by months or quarters; tracking them gives managers time to act before the financial consequences hit
A scorecard with 10-15 measures can focus attention; one with 40 is a report, not a management tool; pick the measures that matter most and revisit them annually
A scorecard disconnected from compensation changes nothing; people do what they are paid to do, and if the compensation system rewards old measures, the new measures will be ignored

class_schedule.topic: Lean Accounting, Throughput Costing, and Contemporary Alternatives
learning_objectives: Explain the lean manufacturing philosophy and its implications for cost accounting
Compute throughput, inventory, and operating expense under the theory of constraints
Contrast absorption costing, variable costing, and throughput costing and identify when each produces different operating income
Evaluate when lean accounting and throughput costing are appropriate alternatives to traditional managerial accounting
readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 6 (variable costing) and Ch. 15 (statement of cash flows)
Maskell, Baggaley & Grasso, Practical Lean Accounting (2nd ed., 2012), Ch. 1-4
Goldratt, The Goal (30th anniversary ed., 2014), Part 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional managerial accounting was built around mass production: high inventories, long runs, batch-and-queue manufacturing, and cost systems that absorb fixed overhead into inventory. When Toyota built the production system that became known as lean, almost every assumption of traditional cost accounting became wrong. Lean production runs at low inventory, small lots, continuous flow, and pull from customer demand. The financial statements produced by absorption costing actively mislead lean managers. When inventory goes down under absorption costing, fixed overhead flows from the balance sheet to the income statement, producing an apparent drop in operating income — exactly when lean improvements are working. The better you get at lean, the worse your reported income looks in the short run, which is a terrible signal. Lean accounting addresses this by using value-stream costing (accumulating costs by value stream, not by product), plain English statements for shop-floor use, and operational metrics that tie directly to lean improvements (cycle time, first-pass yield, inventory days). Throughput costing is a related contemporary alternative developed by Eli Goldratt in his theory of constraints. It treats only direct materials as a unit-level variable cost; labor, overhead, and everything else are operating expense. Throughput is revenue minus direct materials — the rate at which the system generates money through sales. Operating expense is everything else spent to convert inventory to sales. Inventory is money tied up in the system. The three measures — throughput, inventory, operating expense — form the framework for all operational decisions. Decisions that increase throughput, decrease inventory, or decrease operating expense are good; decisions that do not are suspect. The approach is conceptually different from traditional cost accounting and its proponents argue it produces better decisions in constraint-dominated environments. Whether lean accounting and throughput costing are right for you depends on your operations. Firms running lean or in flow-dominated environments benefit from these alternatives. Firms with diverse products, traditional job shops, or service operations may do better with ABC or classical standard costing. The point of this session is that the tools you choose should match the operational reality, not the textbook default.
key_concepts: Lean manufacturing: production system emphasizing waste elimination, continuous flow, pull from customer demand, rapid changeovers, and continuous improvement; originated at Toyota
Value stream: the complete set of activities required to deliver a product or service to a customer; the natural unit of cost accumulation in lean accounting
Value-stream costing: accumulate costs by value stream rather than by product; simpler than ABC, matches lean organization, and focuses attention on waste within the value stream
Plain English financial statements: lean companies often report to shop floors in plain formats avoiding absorption-costing artifacts; emphasizes cash, throughput, and controllable cost
Theory of constraints: framework developed by Goldratt that treats any system as governed by one or a few bottleneck constraints; performance is improved by relieving constraints
Throughput (TOC sense): revenue minus totally variable cost (usually just direct materials); the rate at which the system generates money through sales
Operating expense (TOC sense): all money spent to convert inventory to throughput that is not directly variable; includes labor, overhead, utilities, everything else
Inventory (TOC sense): all money tied up in the system, including raw materials, work in process, finished goods, equipment, buildings

Absorption vs variable vs throughput costing: absorption treats all production costs as product costs; variable treats only variable production costs as product costs; throughput treats only direct materials as product costs; they produce different inventory values and different income when production and sales differ

Reconciliation: the difference in operating income between absorption and variable costing equals the change in inventory times the fixed manufacturing overhead per unit; explains why lean improvements that reduce inventory cause apparent income declines under absorption
lecture_notes.demonstration: A manufacturer produces 10,000 units in year 1 and sells 8,000. Selling price $50, direct materials $20, direct labor $5, variable overhead $3, fixed manufacturing overhead $100,000 ($10/unit at production volume). Fixed selling and admin $40,000. Compute income three ways. Absorption costing: product cost per unit = $20 + $5 + $3 + $10 = $38. Revenue $400,000. COGS = 8,000 × $38 = $304,000. Gross profit $96,000. S&A $40,000. Operating income $56,000. Ending inventory 2,000 × $38 = $76,000. Variable costing: product cost per unit = $20 + $5 + $3 = $28. Revenue $400,000. Variable COGS = 8,000 × $28 = $224,000. Contribution margin $176,000. Fixed manufacturing $100,000. Fixed S&A $40,000. Operating income $36,000. Ending inventory 2,000 × $28 = $56,000. Throughput costing: product cost per unit = $20. Revenue $400,000. Throughput = 8,000 × ($50 - $20) = $240,000. Total operating expense = $5 × 10,000 + $3 × 10,000 + $100,000 + $40,000 = $220,000. Operating income $20,000. Ending inventory 2,000 × $20 = $40,000. Income differs because different amounts of fixed cost are inventoried. Absorption defers $20,000 of fixed overhead in ending inventory. Variable defers nothing. Throughput costs the fewest items to inventory, so more costs hit the period. Now run the reverse: year 2, production 8,000 units, sales 10,000 (drawing down inventory built in year 1). Absorption income drops by the $20,000 of fixed overhead released from inventory; variable and throughput are unaffected by inventory change. Discussion: under lean, where inventory is systematically reduced, absorption costing reports declining income when the operations are actually improving. This is why many lean companies use variable or throughput costing for internal management, even though they still prepare absorption-based GAAP financials for external reporting.
setup_requirements: Excel workbook comparing three costing methods
Reconciliation worksheet linking absorption and variable operating income

review.value: Under absorption costing, building inventory defers fixed overhead to the balance sheet and increases reported income; lean companies that reduce inventory see reported income drop even when operations improve

review.key: Variable Costing Removes the Inventory Distortion
review.value: Variable costing treats fixed manufacturing overhead as a period cost, so reported income depends only on sales and operational efficiency, not on inventory changes; preferred for internal management decisions

Throughput accounting measures throughput (revenue - material), operating expense, and inventory, and evaluates decisions by their effect on these three; most useful in constraint-dominated environments

review.key: Match the Tool to the Operation
review.value: There is no single best costing method; absorption is required for GAAP, variable is best for internal decisions, ABC is best for diverse-product operations, lean and throughput are best for flow operations; managers need fluency in all of them

class_schedule.topic: Synthesis, Case Integration, and Strategic Managerial Accounting
learning_objectives: Integrate costing, decision, control, and performance measurement concepts in a comprehensive case analysis
Diagnose the managerial accounting needs of a business from its operating characteristics
Critique an existing managerial accounting system and recommend improvements
Reflect on the ethical dimensions of managerial accounting and the role of the CMA
readings: Horngren, Datar & Rajan, Cost Accounting (16th ed.), capstone chapters as selected
Kaplan, 'One Cost System Isn't Enough,' Harvard Business Review (Jan-Feb 1988)
Johnson & Kaplan, Relevance Lost (HBS Press, 1987), Ch. 9 - Conclusion

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session integrates the entire course around a single question: how would you design the managerial accounting system for a specific, complicated business? The answer is never a template. It depends on what the business does, where value is created, which decisions matter, and which people need what information. A consulting firm needs job costing down to the hour, profit-per-partner analysis, and utilization metrics. A commodity refiner needs process costing, yield variance analysis, and energy cost tracking. A software-as-a-service company needs unit economics per customer, cohort analysis, and customer-lifetime-value projections — none of which the textbook covered directly but all of which build on the principles you have learned. The Johnson and Kaplan book Relevance Lost argued that by the 1980s American managerial accounting had become captive to financial reporting and had lost its ability to serve operational decisions. Most of what we have studied — ABC, balanced scorecard, lean accounting, throughput costing — emerged as responses to that critique. You will enter a business world in which the default cost systems are still dominated by GAAP-driven absorption costing with simple allocation bases, and in which the opportunity for insight is huge. The managerial accountant who understands when to use which tool, who can diagnose what the current system is missing, and who can design better measures will be genuinely valuable. Ethics is the final layer. The CMA Code of Ethics binds practitioners to competence, confidentiality, integrity, and credibility. In managerial accounting, the ethical pressures are different from financial accounting. Nobody is going to ask a CMA to forge a 10-K filing, but plenty of CMAs will be asked to produce variance reports that let division managers look better than they are, to shift costs across segments in ways that favor one manager at the expense of another, or to bend product costing to support a pricing decision. Each of these is an integrity test. The role of the managerial accountant is not merely to produce numbers — it is to produce numbers that are truthful, that serve good decisions, and that respect the interests of the organization as a whole rather than any particular faction. The course has taught you the techniques. What happens with those techniques when your supervisor asks you to massage them is up to you.
key_concepts: System design depends on operational reality: job vs process, diverse vs homogeneous products, stable vs volatile demand, lean vs mass production — each drives different system choices

Integration of course topics: costing system (what products cost), decision support (should we take this order), planning and control (budget vs actual), performance measurement (are managers hitting their targets) — these four functions must be internally consistent

Relevance Lost thesis: GAAP-driven absorption costing with simple allocation bases misses the operational insights that managerial accounting should provide; contemporary alternatives (ABC, BSC, lean, TOC) exist to recover relevance

Unit economics in subscription businesses: customer acquisition cost (CAC), customer lifetime value (LTV), payback period, contribution margin per cohort — the SaaS version of contribution margin analysis

Cost of quality framework: prevention costs, appraisal costs, internal failure costs, external failure costs; total cost of quality is minimized by investing in prevention before failure costs become large

Target costing: starting from a target selling price dictated by the market and working backwards to a target cost the organization must hit; used by Toyota, Sony, and many consumer products firms in product development
Kaizen costing: systematic small-step cost reduction over the life of a product, using target cost reduction percentages set at the start of each period and measured against achieved cost

Strategic cost management: linking cost analysis to strategic choices about value chain position, product mix, and competitive advantage; treating cost as a strategic rather than purely operational variable
CMA certification: the professional credential for managerial accountants, administered by the IMA; requires passing two exams and meeting work experience requirements

Ethical conflicts unique to managerial accounting: variance reporting pressure, transfer pricing disputes, cost allocation favoritism, strategic pricing analysis, budget negotiation honesty — all situations where the CMA must balance stakeholder pressures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capstone case. Read an abbreviated case on a real firm: a mid-size specialty chemicals manufacturer with three plants, six product families, and sales to both industrial customers and distributors. The firm has grown by acquisition, has three different legacy cost systems from the acquired companies, and has been losing share to a larger competitor. Its ROI has declined for four consecutive quarters. The CFO asks the team to diagnose the managerial accounting system and recommend changes. Students work through the case in small groups using a structured template. Step 1: what decisions does the business need the cost system to support? Pricing (yes, changing market), product mix (yes, declining ROI), plant consolidation (maybe), new product development (yes, need target costing). Step 2: what is the current system doing well or badly? Three different cost systems is automatically a problem — same product could show different costs depending on plant. Allocation base is probably labor hours inherited from one legacy system, inappropriate now that overhead dominates. Step 3: what alternative approaches apply? ABC for diverse-product complexity; lean accounting if the operations are converting to lean; target costing for new product development; balanced scorecard for pulling together customer, process, and financial measures. Step 4: build a recommendation — harmonize the three cost systems into a single ABC structure; introduce value-stream reporting for the plants that are farthest along in lean; add target costing for the R&D pipeline; cascade a balanced scorecard across business units; align compensation with the scorecard. Step 5: anticipate resistance — plant managers will oppose losing their local cost systems, R&D will resist target costing as restricting creativity, sales will resist contribution-margin-based incentive structures. Address each explicitly. Close with a discussion of what happens if the CFO later pressures the team to shade the ABC numbers so that a favored plant looks better than it actually is. How does the team respond? This is where technique, judgment, and ethics all come together.
setup_requirements: Capstone case handout (distributed in previous session)
Diagnosis template covering costing, decisions, control, and performance measurement
IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice

review.value: The right cost system depends on what the business does, what decisions it needs to make, and how its operations work; there is no universal answer, only good fits and bad fits

A simple but well-integrated system usually outperforms a complicated system where different parts work on different assumptions; coherence across costing, decisions, control, and performance is the goal

review.key: Contemporary Tools Exist Because Traditional Tools Failed
review.value: ABC, balanced scorecard, lean accounting, and throughput costing all emerged from specific failures of traditional systems; know which problem each solves so you can choose the right tool for the situation

Managerial accountants face integrity tests in variance reporting, transfer pricing, cost allocation, and budget negotiation; the quality of the numbers depends on the character of the people producing them

quizzes.topic: Cost Concepts, Cost Behavior, and CVP Analysis

questions.question: A maintenance department has the following monthly data: January 4,000 machine hours and $32,000 total cost; July 8,000 machine hours and $48,000 total cost. (a) Apply the high-low method to separate this mixed cost into fixed and variable components. (b) Write the cost function. (c) Predict total cost at 6,500 machine hours. (d) Explain two reasons the high-low prediction might be unreliable and describe what regression analysis would do differently.
questions.answer: (a) Variable rate = ($48,000 - $32,000) / (8,000 - 4,000) = $16,000 / 4,000 = $4 per machine hour. Fixed cost = $48,000 - ($4 × 8,000) = $48,000 - $32,000 = $16,000 per month. (b) Cost function: Y = $16,000 + $4X. (c) Predicted cost at 6,500 hours = $16,000 + ($4 × 6,500) = $16,000 + $26,000 = $42,000. (d) Two reliability concerns: (1) High-low uses only two data points — January and July — which may not be representative if one happens to contain unusual spikes (a machine breakdown, a weather event, a strike). Regression uses all 12 months, so extreme observations influence the line less. (2) Machine hours may not be the best cost driver; if maintenance cost is really driven by a different variable (age of equipment, shift complexity), a regression with that variable will produce better R-squared and more accurate predictions. Regression analysis fits a least-squares line through all data points, reports R-squared showing how much variation the driver explains, and reports standard errors giving a range of prediction uncertainty.

rubric.key: Correct variable rate and fixed cost calculation
Correct explanation of what regression adds

questions.question: A startup subscription-box company charges $60 per month. Variable cost per box is $35. Monthly fixed costs total $100,000. (a) Calculate the monthly break-even volume in boxes. (b) Calculate the monthly margin of safety if actual volume is 5,500 boxes. (c) The founder wants to earn $60,000 monthly after-tax profit at a 25% tax rate. How many boxes must she sell? (d) The company considers raising the price to $70, but forecasts a 10% drop in volume from 5,500 boxes. Should she raise the price?
questions.answer: (a) CM per unit = $60 - $35 = $25. Break-even = $100,000 / $25 = 4,000 boxes per month. (b) Margin of safety in units = 5,500 - 4,000 = 1,500 boxes. As percent of actual sales = 1,500/5,500 = 27.3%. In dollars = 1,500 × $60 = $90,000. (c) After-tax target $60,000; pre-tax target = $60,000 / (1 - 0.25) = $80,000. Required units = ($100,000 + $80,000) / $25 = 7,200 boxes. (d) Current: 5,500 × $25 = $137,500 CM, $37,500 operating income. New: price $70, VC $35, CM $35. Volume 5,500 × 0.9 = 4,950. New CM = 4,950 × $35 = $173,250; new operating income $73,250. Since $73,250 > $37,500, yes raise the price — operating income nearly doubles because the CM increase per unit ($10) more than offsets the volume decline. Still verify qualitative risk: will the 10% estimate hold, or will some customers cancel more aggressively once price rises?

rubric.key: Correct margin of safety in units, dollars, and percent

questions.question: Classify each of the following costs for a regional airline. For each, state: (i) product cost or period cost; (ii) variable, fixed, or mixed; (iii) direct or indirect with respect to a single flight as the cost object. Then explain one classification that would change if the cost object were the airline's overall operations rather than a single flight. (1) Jet fuel burned on a flight. (2) First-officer salary. (3) Aircraft depreciation. (4) Gate agent wages at the airline's hub. (5) Advertising.
questions.answer: (1) JET FUEL: product cost, variable (with flight distance/duration), direct (traceable to the specific flight). (2) FIRST-OFFICER SALARY: product cost, mostly fixed monthly salary, indirect with respect to a single flight (first officer works multiple flights per month and salary is not per-flight). (3) AIRCRAFT DEPRECIATION: product cost, fixed, indirect to a single flight (straight-line allocated over useful life regardless of flights). (4) GATE AGENT WAGES: product cost (if serving the flight) or period cost depending on argument; fixed (hourly but scheduled by shift, not flight volume); indirect to a single flight (agents handle many flights per shift). (5) ADVERTISING: period cost, fixed in the short run, indirect to any single flight. CHANGE IN CLASSIFICATION: with airline operations as the whole cost object, aircraft depreciation becomes a direct cost (traceable to the operation as a unit) and first-officer salary becomes direct. Traceability depends on the cost object granularity, not on the cost itself. This is a common exam trap.

rubric.key: Correct classification across all five costs
Correct analysis of classification change with cost object
Explanation acknowledges traceability depends on cost object

quizzes.topic: Job-Order Costing, Process Costing, and ABC

questions.question: A custom print shop uses job-order costing. At the start of the year, estimated manufacturing overhead was $180,000 and estimated direct labor hours were 15,000. During Job 742, direct materials cost $3,200, direct labor was 48 hours at $24 per hour, and machine time was 12 hours. (a) Calculate the predetermined overhead rate. (b) Apply overhead to Job 742. (c) Calculate the total cost of Job 742. (d) If Job 742 was bid at $7,200, calculate gross margin percentage. (e) Suppose the shop had used machine hours instead of labor hours as the allocation base (estimated machine hours 6,000). What would Job 742's overhead be? Discuss which allocation base is more appropriate.
questions.answer: (a) POHR (labor hours) = $180,000 / 15,000 = $12 per direct labor hour. (b) Overhead applied to Job 742 = 48 hours × $12 = $576. (c) Total cost = DM $3,200 + DL (48 × $24 = $1,152) + OH $576 = $4,928. (d) Gross margin = $7,200 - $4,928 = $2,272. GM% = $2,272/$7,200 = 31.6%. (e) Alternative rate = $180,000 / 6,000 = $30 per machine hour. Overhead at 12 machine hours = $360. ANALYSIS: Labor-based rate ($576) and machine-based rate ($360) differ by $216, producing different unit costs. Machine hours are more appropriate if overhead is driven by equipment use (depreciation, machine maintenance, power). Labor hours are more appropriate if overhead is driven by labor-related activities (supervision, labor fringe benefits, setup). A print shop's overhead likely includes both, so a two-stage or ABC approach might be more accurate. The key issue is whether the allocation base causally drives overhead consumption.

rubric.key: Correct applied overhead and total cost

questions.question: A cereal manufacturer uses process costing in its Mixing Department. Beginning WIP: 8,000 pounds, 100% materials complete, 30% conversion complete; costs $12,000 materials + $6,000 conversion. Started in April: 40,000 pounds. Completed and transferred out: 42,000 pounds. Ending WIP: 6,000 pounds, 100% materials, 60% conversion. April costs added: $80,000 materials + $72,000 conversion. Use the weighted-average method to (a) calculate equivalent units for materials and conversion, (b) calculate cost per equivalent unit for each, (c) assign costs to units transferred out and ending WIP, (d) verify the total costs to account for tie to cost assignment.
questions.answer: (a) EQUIVALENT UNITS: Materials = 42,000 (completed) + 6,000 × 100% (ending WIP) = 48,000 units. Conversion = 42,000 + 6,000 × 60% = 42,000 + 3,600 = 45,600 units. (b) COST PER EQUIVALENT UNIT: Materials = ($12,000 + $80,000) / 48,000 = $92,000 / 48,000 = $1.9167 per unit. Conversion = ($6,000 + $72,000) / 45,600 = $78,000 / 45,600 = $1.7105 per unit. Total cost per equivalent unit = $3.6272. (c) COST ASSIGNMENT: Transferred out = 42,000 × $3.6272 = $152,342. Ending WIP materials = 6,000 × $1.9167 = $11,500. Ending WIP conversion = 3,600 × $1.7105 = $6,158. Total ending WIP = $17,658. Total assigned = $152,342 + $17,658 = $170,000. (d) VERIFICATION: Total costs to account for = beginning WIP ($12,000 + $6,000) + April costs ($80,000 + $72,000) = $18,000 + $152,000 = $170,000. Matches.

rubric.key: Correct equivalent units for materials and conversion
Correct cost assignment to transferred out and ending WIP

questions.question: A factory has $800,000 in manufacturing overhead and produces two products. Standard (12,000 units/year) uses 1 labor hour and 2 machine hours per unit. Deluxe (2,000 units/year) uses 3 labor hours and 4 machine hours per unit. Traditional costing uses labor hours. ABC identifies four activities: setups ($200,000; 100 setups total — Standard 20, Deluxe 80); machining ($240,000; 32,000 machine hours — Standard 24,000, Deluxe 8,000); inspection ($120,000; 600 inspections — Standard 200, Deluxe 400); general plant ($240,000; 18,000 labor hours — Standard 12,000, Deluxe 6,000). Calculate total overhead per unit for each product under (a) traditional labor-hour allocation and (b) ABC. Which product is 'cross-subsidized' under traditional costing?
questions.answer: (a) TRADITIONAL: Total labor hours = 12,000 + 6,000 = 18,000. Rate = $800,000/18,000 = $44.44/hr. Standard OH = 1 × $44.44 = $44.44/unit. Deluxe OH = 3 × $44.44 = $133.33/unit. (b) ABC: Activity rates — setups $200,000/100 = $2,000 per setup; machining $240,000/32,000 = $7.50 per machine hour; inspection $120,000/600 = $200 per inspection; general plant $240,000/18,000 = $13.33 per labor hour. STANDARD overhead: 20 × $2,000 = $40,000; 24,000 × $7.50 = $180,000; 200 × $200 = $40,000; 12,000 × $13.33 = $160,000. Total = $420,000 / 12,000 units = $35/unit. DELUXE overhead: 80 × $2,000 = $160,000; 8,000 × $7.50 = $60,000; 400 × $200 = $80,000; 6,000 × $13.33 = $80,000. Total = $380,000 / 2,000 units = $190/unit. CROSS-SUBSIDY: Traditional shows Standard at $44.44 (overcharged by $9.44 relative to ABC's $35) and Deluxe at $133.33 (undercharged by $56.67 relative to ABC's $190). Standard is cross-subsidizing Deluxe — high-volume simple product bearing costs for low-volume complex product. Classic ABC pattern: complex low-volume products consume disproportionate setup, inspection, and engineering resources that labor-hour allocation misses. Total overhead is the same ($420K + $380K = $800K) — ABC redistributes rather than finding new money.

rubric.key: Correct traditional overhead per unit for both products
Correct ABC overhead per unit for both products
Correct identification of cross-subsidization with explanation

questions.question: A firm forecasts unit sales of 5,000 (April), 6,000 (May), 7,000 (June), and 8,000 (July). Desired ending inventory is 20% of next month's sales. Beginning inventory on April 1 is 900 units. Each unit requires 3 pounds of direct materials at $4/pound, and desired ending materials inventory is 15% of next month's production needs. (a) Prepare the production budget for April, May, and June. (b) Prepare the direct materials purchases budget for April and May. (c) If the firm pays 60% of materials purchases in the month of purchase and 40% the following month, what is the cash disbursement for materials in May?
questions.answer: (a) PRODUCTION BUDGET. April: sales 5,000 + desired ending inventory (20% × May 6,000) 1,200 - beginning inventory 900 = 5,300 units. May: 6,000 + (20% × 7,000) 1,400 - 1,200 = 6,200 units. June: 7,000 + (20% × 8,000) 1,600 - 1,400 = 7,200 units. (b) DIRECT MATERIALS PURCHASES BUDGET. April materials needed for production: 5,300 × 3 = 15,900 lbs. Desired ending materials inventory: 15% × May production 6,200 × 3 = 2,790 lbs. Beginning materials inventory (15% × April 5,300 × 3 = 2,385 lbs). April purchases = 15,900 + 2,790 - 2,385 = 16,305 lbs × $4 = $65,220. May: production needs 6,200 × 3 = 18,600 lbs. Ending inventory: 15% × June 7,200 × 3 = 3,240 lbs. Beginning inventory: 2,790 lbs. May purchases = 18,600 + 3,240 - 2,790 = 19,050 lbs × $4 = $76,200. (c) MAY CASH DISBURSEMENTS = 60% × May $76,200 + 40% × April $65,220 = $45,720 + $26,088 = $71,808.

rubric.key: Correct production budget for three months
Correct materials purchases budget for April and May

questions.question: A company's standard for one widget is 4 pounds of material at $3/lb and 2 hours of labor at $18/hr. During March, 1,000 widgets were produced. Actual results: purchased and used 4,200 lbs at $2.85/lb; actual labor 2,050 hours at $19/hr. (a) Calculate materials price and quantity variances. (b) Calculate labor rate and efficiency variances. (c) For each variance, state whether favorable or unfavorable and suggest a plausible root cause. (d) Explain one way these variances might be interrelated.
questions.answer: (a) MATERIALS. Price variance = (AP - SP) × AQ = ($2.85 - $3) × 4,200 = -$0.15 × 4,200 = -$630 = $630 Favorable (F). Quantity variance = (AQ - SQ) × SP = (4,200 - 4,000) × $3 = 200 × $3 = $600 Unfavorable (U). Standard quantity = 1,000 × 4 = 4,000 lbs. Net materials variance = $630F - $600U = $30F. (b) LABOR. Rate variance = (AR - SR) × AH = ($19 - $18) × 2,050 = $1 × 2,050 = $2,050 U. Efficiency variance = (AH - SH) × SR = (2,050 - 2,000) × $18 = 50 × $18 = $900 U. Standard hours = 1,000 × 2 = 2,000. Net labor variance = $2,050U + $900U = $2,950 U. (c) ROOT CAUSES. Materials price $630F: purchased cheaper material, possibly lower grade or bulk discount. Materials quantity $600U: used more material than standard, possibly waste from lower-grade material or inexperienced workers. Labor rate $2,050U: paid above standard wage, likely overtime or higher-skilled workers covering shifts. Labor efficiency $900U: took longer than standard, possibly caused by material problems or inexperienced workers. (d) INTERRELATION: The cheaper material (price F) may have more defects or be harder to work with, causing both higher usage (quantity U) and slower production (efficiency U). The net materials favorable $30 is dwarfed by labor unfavorable $2,950 — the 'savings' from cheaper material triggered much larger losses in labor. This is a classic trade-off that purchasing departments can create when they are evaluated solely on price variance without considering downstream effects. The lesson: interdependence means variances must be analyzed together, not in isolation.

rubric.key: Root cause explanations for each variance

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the flexible budget and why comparing actual results to a static budget produces misleading performance evaluation. Give a specific numerical example involving budgeted sales of 10,000 units with actual sales of 12,000 units.
questions.answer: PURPOSE: The flexible budget adjusts the original (static) budget to the actual level of activity. It answers the question: 'What should costs and profits have been at the actual volume produced?' Without flexing, any performance comparison mixes volume effects (sales were higher or lower than planned) with efficiency effects (we operated better or worse than planned at whatever volume we had). These are two different management issues and should be separated. EXAMPLE: Static budget assumes 10,000 units at $20 price, $12 variable cost, $50,000 fixed cost. Static budgeted revenue = $200,000; variable cost = $120,000; contribution margin = $80,000; operating income = $30,000. Actual: 12,000 units, actual revenue $252,000, actual variable cost $156,000, actual fixed $52,000. STATIC COMPARISON: Actual revenue $252,000 vs static $200,000 = $52,000 favorable. But this comparison mixes two things: (1) we sold 2,000 extra units (volume), and (2) we charged $21/unit rather than $20 (price). FLEXIBLE BUDGET adjusts for the 12,000-unit volume: flexible revenue = 12,000 × $20 = $240,000; flexible variable cost = 12,000 × $12 = $144,000; flexible contribution margin = $96,000; flexible fixed = $50,000; flexible operating income = $46,000. Now comparisons are clean. Sales volume variance: static $30,000 to flexible $46,000 = $16,000 favorable (from selling more). Sales price variance: flexible $240,000 to actual $252,000 revenue = $12,000 favorable (from higher-than-standard pricing). Variable cost flexible budget variance: flexible $144,000 to actual $156,000 = $12,000 unfavorable. Fixed cost flexible budget variance: flexible $50,000 to actual $52,000 = $2,000 unfavorable. Total variance still adds up, but now management can see that volume drove most of the good news, while operating efficiency actually declined. Holding the operating manager responsible for the static variance is wrong — the volume variance is likely a sales/marketing issue, and the cost variances reflect operating efficiency at actual volume.

rubric.key: Correct numerical example with static comparison
Clean separation of volume and efficiency variances

questions.question: A company currently makes component Z internally at the following per-unit cost: direct materials $10, direct labor $8, variable overhead $4, allocated fixed overhead $12. Annual production: 10,000 units. A supplier offers to sell Z for $30 per unit. Of the allocated fixed overhead, 40% is truly avoidable if production is outsourced (eliminated supervisors, some utilities); the other 60% is unavoidable (plant overhead that continues regardless). (a) Should the company make or buy? Show the incremental analysis. (b) Now suppose the freed production capacity could be used to manufacture a new product generating $25,000 additional contribution margin per year. How does this change the decision? (c) List three qualitative factors that might override the purely financial answer.
questions.answer: (a) MAKE-OR-BUY ANALYSIS. Full 'textbook' cost per unit = $10 + $8 + $4 + $12 = $34. Supplier price $30. Naive answer: buy. But only AVOIDABLE costs are relevant. Avoidable per unit = $10 DM + $8 DL + $4 VOH + ($12 × 40%) avoidable fixed = $10 + $8 + $4 + $4.80 = $26.80. Supplier $30. Cost to buy > cost to make ($30 > $26.80). RECOMMENDATION: Make. Annual savings from continuing to make = ($30 - $26.80) × 10,000 = $32,000. (b) OPPORTUNITY COST. If capacity could earn $25,000 elsewhere, add this to make cost as opportunity cost. Adjusted make cost = ($26.80 × 10,000) + $25,000 = $268,000 + $25,000 = $293,000. Buy cost = $30 × 10,000 = $300,000. Still make — savings $7,000. If the alternative contribution were $35,000, then make cost becomes $303,000 > $300,000 buy cost, and buy becomes cheaper. (c) QUALITATIVE FACTORS: (1) Quality control — can supplier meet specifications reliably? (2) Supply chain risk — what happens if the supplier's plant shuts down, prices rise, or supplier goes bankrupt? Dell/Asus lesson: outsourcing critical components can create strategic vulnerability. (3) Capability erosion — if the company stops making Z, it loses the skills and equipment to restart if needed; this reduces flexibility. (4) Employee impact — layoffs from outsourcing affect morale and recruiting. (5) Strategic fit — does Z represent core competency that should remain in-house?

rubric.key: Correct identification of avoidable vs unavoidable costs

questions.question: Consider a 5-year capital project requiring $400,000 initial investment and generating cash inflows of $120,000 per year. Required rate of return is 10%. (a) Calculate NPV using a PV annuity factor of 3.7908. (b) Calculate simple payback period. (c) Calculate profitability index. (d) Suppose the annual cash inflow in year 3 is reduced to $60,000 while other years stay at $120,000. Recalculate NPV using individual PV factors (Year 1: 0.9091; Year 2: 0.8264; Year 3: 0.7513; Year 4: 0.6830; Year 5: 0.6209). Should the project still be accepted?
questions.answer: (a) NPV BASE CASE: PV of $120,000 annuity = $120,000 × 3.7908 = $454,896. NPV = $454,896 - $400,000 = $54,896 positive. Accept. (b) PAYBACK = $400,000 / $120,000 = 3.33 years. (c) PROFITABILITY INDEX = $454,896 / $400,000 = 1.137. Above 1 — accept. (d) REVISED NPV: Year 1 $120,000 × 0.9091 = $109,092; Year 2 $120,000 × 0.8264 = $99,168; Year 3 $60,000 × 0.7513 = $45,078; Year 4 $120,000 × 0.6830 = $81,960; Year 5 $120,000 × 0.6209 = $74,508. Total PV = $409,806. Revised NPV = $409,806 - $400,000 = $9,806. Still positive but barely — accept with caution. Sensitivity insight: a single $60,000 year-3 dip reduced NPV from $54,896 to $9,806 (a $45,090 swing). Further downside risk could push NPV negative. Before committing, management should analyze: (1) how likely is the year 3 scenario? (2) what factors could cause further declines? (3) what is the downside scenario (all bad years)? Responsible capital budgeting never stops at a single point estimate when cash flows are uncertain.

rubric.key: Correct revised NPV with individual PV factors

questions.question: Your company has idle capacity and receives a special order for 5,000 units at $22/unit. Regular products sell for $35. Unit variable cost is $18; unit fixed cost allocated is $10. The special order requires no additional fixed costs and will not affect regular market pricing. (a) Should the order be accepted? Show calculation. (b) How would the answer change if accepting the order requires displacing 2,000 units of regular sales? (c) Explain why idle capacity is the critical condition for the analysis.
questions.answer: (a) IDLE CAPACITY. Relevant cost per unit = variable cost $18 (fixed cost is irrelevant because it is unchanged). Incremental revenue = 5,000 × $22 = $110,000. Incremental cost = 5,000 × $18 = $90,000. Incremental profit = $20,000. Accept — the order generates $20,000 of contribution margin with no additional fixed cost. (b) WITH DISPLACEMENT. Now accepting 5,000 special order units requires giving up 2,000 regular sales at $35 price with $18 variable cost. Lost contribution margin on displaced sales = 2,000 × ($35 - $18) = 2,000 × $17 = $34,000. Net effect = $20,000 special order contribution - $34,000 lost contribution = -$14,000. Reject — the opportunity cost exceeds the special order benefit. (c) IDLE CAPACITY IS CRITICAL: When capacity is idle, producing for the special order uses resources that would otherwise earn nothing. Fixed costs are sunk and irrelevant — they continue whether we accept or not. So only incremental variable costs matter. When capacity is full, every special-order unit displaces a regular-order unit, and the opportunity cost of that displacement is the contribution margin foregone. The same order is valuable with idle capacity and destructive without it. This is why 'should we accept this order?' always depends on 'what else could we be doing with the capacity?' A secondary issue: even with idle capacity, accepting below-regular pricing risks damaging the regular market if the special price leaks — large B2B customers actively probe for pricing concessions and pass them to other customers. Strategic pricing considers this leakage risk.

rubric.key: Correct displacement analysis with opportunity cost
Clear explanation of why capacity matters

quizzes.topic: Performance Measurement, Transfer Pricing, and Synthesis

questions.question: A division has operating income of $4.8 million on operating assets of $24 million. The firm requires 12% return on assets. (a) Calculate division ROI and residual income. (b) The division has a new project opportunity requiring $5 million investment and generating $800,000 operating income. Calculate the post-project ROI and residual income. (c) Would a ROI-evaluated manager accept the project? Would a residual-income-evaluated manager accept it? Explain the incentive difference and which behavior is better for the firm overall.
questions.answer: (a) CURRENT: ROI = $4,800,000 / $24,000,000 = 20%. Residual income = $4,800,000 - (12% × $24,000,000) = $4,800,000 - $2,880,000 = $1,920,000. (b) POST-PROJECT: New operating income = $4.8M + $0.8M = $5.6M. New assets = $24M + $5M = $29M. New ROI = $5.6M / $29M = 19.31% (declined from 20%). New residual income = $5.6M - (12% × $29M) = $5.6M - $3.48M = $2.12M (increased from $1.92M). Project alone: ROI 16% ($0.8M/$5M), residual income $200,000 ($0.8M - $0.6M). (c) INCENTIVE ANALYSIS. ROI-EVALUATED manager: REJECTS the project because it drags division ROI from 20% to 19.31%. Manager's bonus, reputation, and ranking are based on ROI, so accepting a value-adding project that dilutes the measure is personally damaging. RI-EVALUATED manager: ACCEPTS the project because residual income rises from $1.92M to $2.12M (an increase of $200,000, exactly the project's own residual income contribution). FIRM PERSPECTIVE: The project earns 16% on investment while the firm's required return is 12% — it creates value of $200,000 in residual income annually. The firm benefits from accepting it. ROI produces the WRONG decision. This is the classic problem with ROI as a performance measure: it discourages profitable investment in high-ROI divisions and encourages unprofitable investment in low-ROI divisions. GE under Jack Welch was partly praised and partly criticized for its ROI-like emphasis; EVA and residual income have gained popularity as alternatives precisely because they avoid this trap.

rubric.key: Correct current ROI and residual income
Correct post-project ROI and residual income
Correct prediction of ROI vs RI manager decisions

questions.question: Division A produces a component at variable cost $40 and has idle capacity. Division B currently buys the component externally for $75 and needs 3,000 units/year. The company has a policy of market-based transfer pricing, requiring A to charge $75. (a) Calculate the firm-level effect if B buys internally at $75 instead of externally. (b) Would Division A manager accept a transfer price of $55? Would Division B manager accept $55? What about $60? (c) What transfer price should the firm choose if A has idle capacity vs full capacity?
questions.answer: (a) FIRM EFFECT WITH MARKET PRICE $75: B saves $0 (pays $75 externally or internally). A earns $75 × 3,000 = $225,000 revenue internally, with variable cost $40 × 3,000 = $120,000; A's contribution from internal transfer = $105,000. External customer sale opportunity: zero (idle capacity). The firm-level effect is clearer by recognizing: external purchase sends $225,000 to outside supplier; internal purchase keeps that cash inside. The savings equal the difference between what would have been paid outside ($225,000) and the firm's own incremental cost ($120,000), which is $105,000. (b) AT $55: A's contribution = ($55 - $40) × 3,000 = $45,000 (still positive with idle capacity). A manager accepts. B saves $20/unit vs external $75 = $60,000 total. B manager loves it. $55 is mutually acceptable. AT $60: A earns ($60-$40) × 3,000 = $60,000. B saves $15/unit = $45,000. Both accept. Any price between $40 (A's floor) and $75 (B's ceiling) is mutually acceptable. (c) IDLE CAPACITY: Optimal floor is variable cost $40 plus some markup to motivate A manager. $55-$65 range reasonable. FULL CAPACITY: A's floor is variable cost PLUS opportunity cost of foregone external sales. If A can sell all output at $75 external, opportunity cost = $35 per unit, so minimum acceptable transfer = $40 + $35 = $75 = market price. The market price rule becomes the right answer only at full capacity — hence the classical result: use market price when market-priced external alternatives exist and capacity is fully utilized. At idle capacity, market price is too high and will cause underutilization of internal capabilities.

rubric.key: Correct reasoning about idle vs full capacity

questions.question: Your CFO is concerned that the company's balanced scorecard has 38 measures and seems to have little effect on behavior. Diagnose the problem and recommend three specific improvements. Reference a real company that has used the scorecard effectively or unsuccessfully.
questions.answer: DIAGNOSIS. Thirty-eight measures is far beyond the 10-15 range recommended by Kaplan and Norton and most practitioners. Problems: (1) attention dilution — no one can focus on 38 things simultaneously; most will be ignored; (2) metric gaming becomes easier when no single measure is watched closely; (3) the scorecard becomes a compliance report rather than a management tool; (4) likely no cause-and-effect linkages have been traced across measures — adding more measures was the response to political pressure from every function wanting its metric on the scorecard. THREE IMPROVEMENTS: (1) STRIP TO 12-15 MEASURES. Identify the three or four per perspective that most directly capture strategic priorities. Kill the rest. Every measure that survives must earn its place by being tied to a cause-and-effect hypothesis linking learning/growth to process to customer to financial. (2) DRAW A STRATEGY MAP. Each measure must have a documented hypothesis for how it causally affects downstream measures. For example: 'Training hours per rep (L&G) drives shorter sales cycle (process), which drives higher deal close rate (customer), which drives new customer revenue (financial).' Test the hypotheses with data over time; if the correlations don't appear, either the hypothesis is wrong or the measures are wrong. (3) ALIGN COMPENSATION. The most common failure of balanced scorecards is that the formal performance measurement system differs from the compensation system. Salespeople get paid on revenue; the scorecard measures customer satisfaction; nobody cares about customer satisfaction. Fix: rebase compensation so that the scorecard measures actually affect bonuses and performance reviews. Without alignment, the scorecard is theater. REAL COMPANIES. MOBIL NORTH AMERICA (1990s) used a balanced scorecard to turn around its downstream business; Kaplan documents the case. Key was cascading the scorecard down to each service station and aligning compensation. HILTON used a scorecard to coordinate strategy across hundreds of properties in the late 1990s and 2000s with similar success. Many implementations have failed quietly: one study found that about 30-40% of scorecard implementations were abandoned within three years, usually because they were treated as reports rather than management tools.

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis of measure-count and attention problems

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Master Budget Project
major_assignments.description: Build a complete master budget for a fictional manufacturing firm across one fiscal year (four quarters). You will receive a firm profile including forecasted unit sales, selling prices, cost standards, operating asset values, desired inventory policies, and financing terms. You must construct the sales budget, production budget, direct materials budget, direct labor budget, manufacturing overhead budget, selling and administrative budget, cash budget, budgeted income statement, and budgeted balance sheet. The project integrates weeks 1-7.
objectives: Demonstrate command of budget mechanics across all operational areas
Produce a coherent, internally consistent master budget
Identify cash shortfalls and propose financing solutions
Analyze and explain key assumptions and their sensitivity
requirements: Complete master budget workbook in Excel with formulas, not hardcoded numbers
Sales, production, materials, labor, overhead, S&A budgets at quarterly level
Cash budget showing cash receipts, disbursements, and required borrowing at quarter ends
Budgeted income statement and balance sheet for year end
Written executive summary (2 pages) explaining key risks and assumptions
Sensitivity analysis table showing the effect of a 10% sales decline on year-end net income and cash
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on accuracy of budget components, internal consistency, clarity of executive summary, and insight of sensitivity analysis. Formula errors and hardcoded numbers lose points. Incoherent budgets (where output of one component does not feed correctly into the next) lose points disproportionally.

rubric.key: Sales and production budgets accurate and consistent
Direct materials and labor budgets correct
Overhead, S&A, and cash budgets with correct timing
Budgeted income statement and balance sheet
Executive summary quality and assumptions discussion

major_assignments.title: Activity-Based Costing Case Analysis
major_assignments.description: Read one of the assigned ABC case studies (Harley-Davidson, Fogler Library System, John Deere Component Works, or Destin Brass) and complete a detailed ABC analysis showing how the alternative cost system would produce different product or service costs from the traditional system. Your analysis must include cost pool identification, cost driver selection, two-stage allocation calculations, and a discussion of managerial implications.
objectives: Apply activity-based costing to a realistic multi-product business
Compare traditional and ABC results and explain the source of differences
Draw managerial implications from the cost distortion revealed by ABC
Evaluate implementation trade-offs including data collection cost
requirements: Complete ABC calculation showing all cost pools, drivers, rates, and product costs
Side-by-side comparison of traditional and ABC product costs
Analysis of which products are cross-subsidized and why
Specific managerial recommendations including pricing, product mix, and process redesign
Discussion of implementation challenges the case company would face
deliverables: Supporting Excel workbook with all ABC calculations
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on calculation accuracy, depth of interpretation (explaining why the differences exist rather than just reporting them), quality of managerial recommendations, and critical engagement with implementation issues.

rubric.key: Substantive analysis of cost distortion sources

major_assignments.title: Capital Budgeting Project Recommendation
major_assignments.description: You are the CFO of a mid-size manufacturer evaluating a $3 million capital investment in new production equipment. You will receive a project profile including projected cash flows, risks, and strategic context. Your assignment is to produce a recommendation memo using NPV, IRR, payback, and profitability index, with sensitivity analysis on key assumptions and a qualitative discussion of non-financial factors. The memo must be ready for the CEO and board.
objectives: Apply capital budgeting techniques to a realistic investment decision
Conduct sensitivity and scenario analysis on key inputs
Integrate quantitative and qualitative factors in a recommendation
requirements: 3-4 page recommendation memo in professional format
NPV, IRR, discounted payback, and profitability index calculations
Sensitivity analysis on revenue, cost, discount rate, and useful life (minimum 4 scenarios)
Scenario analysis: best case, base case, worst case
Qualitative discussion of strategic, operational, and risk factors
Supporting Excel workbook with all calculations and sensitivity tables
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on calculation accuracy, depth of sensitivity and scenario analysis, integration of qualitative factors, clarity of recommendation logic, and professional communication quality. A memo that relies only on NPV without considering scenarios or qualitative factors will lose significant points; a memo that handwaves calculations will lose equally significant points.

rubric.key: NPV, IRR, payback, PI calculations correct

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 6, 9, 12, 15) covering cost concepts and behavior, CVP, job and process costing, ABC, budgeting, variance analysis, relevant costs, capital budgeting, transfer pricing, and synthesis

class_participation.description: Active participation in case discussions, numerical walkthroughs, and synthesis sessions

major_assignments.description: Three major assignments: master budget project (100 pts), ABC case analysis (100 pts), capital budgeting recommendation (100 pts)

midterm_examination.description: Examination covering weeks 1-7: cost concepts, cost behavior, CVP analysis, job and process costing, ABC, and master budgeting

final_examination.description: Comprehensive examination emphasizing second-half content: variance analysis, relevant costs, capital budgeting, responsibility accounting, transfer pricing, balanced scorecard, and strategic managerial accounting

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at every session. Problem walkthroughs and case discussions build cumulatively, and missing a session creates real gaps in skill. Two unexcused absences are allowed; further absences reduce participation grade. Students are responsible for all missed material.
course_policies.late_submissions: Quizzes must be taken during the scheduled session; makeups require documented excuse and must be completed within one week. Major assignments are due at the start of class on the due date; late penalty is 10% per calendar day for up to three days, then zero credit. The master budget project and ABC analysis require significant time and cannot be completed the night before.
course_policies.collaboration: Quiz answers must be individual and closed-book. The master budget project may be discussed at a high level with classmates, but each student must build and submit her own workbook. The ABC case analysis is individual. The capital budgeting memo is individual. Unauthorized collaboration is plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Use of AI tools for calculation work on quizzes and examinations is prohibited. AI as a research aid on major assignments (explaining a concept, generating an outline) is permitted but must be disclosed. Substantially AI-generated work is plagiarism. All sources — textbook, case materials, outside articles, AI tools — must be cited. The Institute of Management Accountants' ethical standards are introduced in week 1 and apply to your conduct throughout the course.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with Excel (full desktop version, not web; mobile Excel is insufficient for formula work). Financial calculator recommended. Access to SEC EDGAR for real 10-K filings used in demonstrations. Textbook (Garrison/Noreen/Brewer or Horngren/Datar/Rajan) required. Optional software: QuickBooks Online student access for job costing reinforcement.
